entries.prompt: Walk me through a moment from the past few days where you said something — out loud or to yourself — that surprised you a little when you heard it back. What was happening, and what do you think you actually meant?
entries.content:
Wednesday, late, and Sam was making that pasta with the lemon. I was at the counter half-watching, half-scrolling, and they asked how the meeting with Mei had gone. Just casually, the way they ask. And I heard myself say, "Fine. I think she's finally figuring out I'm not as smart as she thought I was."

Then I laughed, like it was a joke. Sam didn't laugh. They turned the burner down and looked at me for a second longer than they needed to and said, "That's a wild sentence." And went back to the pasta.

I've been turning it over for two days now.

The thing is, I don't think I meant it as a joke. I think it came out as a joke because that's the only safe shape it could take in the kitchen at 8pm on a weeknight with someone I love about to feed me. But I said it, and the sentence itself was waiting fully formed. It didn't need to be assembled. It was already a finished thought I'd apparently been carrying around.

What had actually happened in the meeting: nothing. Mei gave me feedback on the doc — some things she liked, some questions, one fairly small redirect on the framing of the second section. I'd said, "Yeah, that makes sense, I'll rework it." Walked back to my desk. Got coffee. Reworked it. Sent it back. She said, "Much better, thanks." That's it. That's the whole encounter. By any reasonable measure, that was a normal, productive exchange between two adults who work together.

But somewhere between her saying "I'd push on the framing of section two" and me getting back to my desk, a whole second conversation had run in parallel. *She's noticing. She thought you were sharper than this. The first few months were a fluke and now she's recalibrating. This is the beginning of her revising her opinion downward.* Not in those words exactly. More like a feeling with a shape. And that feeling apparently had enough gravity that hours later, when Sam asked an innocent question, the feeling came out wearing a sentence.

The part that's bothering me — or, fine, the part that I keep poking at like a sore tooth — is the phrase *finally figuring out*. As if there's a true verdict and it's bad and the only question is when other people will catch up to it. That's the thing I want to look at and also don't.

Because here's what I know to be true on paper: I'm good at my job. Mei has told me directly, more than once, that she likes working with me. I got a raise eight months ago. Devon asks for my read on things. None of this is a hallucination. And yet none of it really lands. It sits on the surface of me like water on a waxed car. The sentence "she's finally figuring out I'm not as smart as she thought I was" — that one slid right in. That one fits.

I think what I actually meant, if I'm being honest at the kitchen table with myself, is something like: I am exhausting myself trying to be the version of me that I think Mei first met, and every piece of feedback feels like proof that I'm slipping out of character. The performance is getting harder to maintain. And I'm scared of the moment she sees through it, even though I can't actually articulate what "it" is or what's underneath. Just less, somehow. Smaller. A person who got lucky.

This is so embarrassing to write down. The part of me that runs the meetings and writes the docs is reading this over my shoulder going, *come on. You are 31. You have done this work for nine years. Stop.*

But that's the other thing I noticed about the sentence. The voice of it is young. "Not as smart as she thought I was" is not how I'd describe a colleague's situation, ever. If Devon said that to me about himself I'd think, oh honey, no. I'd be so kind about it. I'd point out all the evidence to the contrary. I'd be patient. The voice I use on myself is one I would never, ever use on another person, and I keep almost-noticing this and then sliding past it because actually addressing it feels like a much bigger project than I have energy for on a Wednesday.

Sam didn't push. They just made the pasta and we ate it and watched something. But before bed they said, "For the record, I don't think Mei is figuring anything out. I think she likes you." And I said "yeah, I know," in the voice you use when you don't, fully, but you appreciate being told.

I keep coming back to the laugh. I laughed when I said it. That's the part I want to remember. The laugh was the costume the sentence wore so it could walk through the kitchen unchallenged. If I hadn't laughed, Sam would have asked a real question and I would have had to either lie or actually say something true, and I'm not sure I had a true sentence ready. I had the bitter joke and then under it, fog.

Maybe that's what I actually meant. Not the content of the sentence — the content is just the usual nonsense, the thing I've apparently been telling myself in some form for years. What I meant, by saying it out loud and laughing, was: *something is loud in here and I don't have a way to talk about it yet, so here's a joke-shaped version, please don't make me say more.*

And Sam, bless them, didn't. But they also didn't laugh. Which is its own kind of answer.

I should call Riley this weekend. I keep saying that. I keep not doing it. That's a different entry.

entries.prompt: Think about a time recently when someone gave you a compliment or said something kind about your work. What did you do with it in the moment, and what did you do with it after?
entries.content:
Friday afternoon, end-of-week sync, and Devon said in front of everyone that the framing doc I'd put together "honestly unlocked the whole conversation for me, I've been stuck on this for weeks." He said it warmly. He said it without looking at me, the way you say something true and slightly inconvenient, like he wasn't performing a kindness, just reporting a fact.

What I did in the moment: I said "oh, thanks, I'm glad it was useful," in the exact tone I'd use if someone told me they liked my sweater. Pleasant. Brisk. Already moving on. I think I actually waved my hand a little, like brushing a fly. And then I immediately said, "though honestly a lot of it came out of that thing you sent me last month, I was just rearranging," which was — let me be precise here — not really true. I had read the thing he sent. It was tangentially related. It was not the source of what I wrote. But in the second between hearing the compliment and needing to respond to it, my mouth produced a sentence that handed the credit back to him. Like a hot potato. Like I couldn't hold it.

Mei was on the call. I don't know if that mattered or if I would have done it anyway. Probably I would have done it anyway.

Then the meeting moved on and I sat there feeling — what's the word. Not pleased. Not warmed. A little embarrassed, actually. Like Devon had said something slightly too generous in public and now everyone had to politely metabolize it and move on. As if his compliment were the awkward thing in the room and not, you know, a normal sentence one coworker says about another's work.

After the meeting I closed my laptop and went and made coffee. Stood at the counter while it dripped. And what I noticed was that I was already replaying the thing he said, but in a specific way — I was scanning it for whether he'd meant it, or whether he was being nice, or whether he was setting up something else. Like I was running diagnostics on his sentence to see if it was safe to keep. *Did he say it because he actually thought it, or did he say it because he wanted to be the kind of person who says generous things in meetings?* That's the question my brain produced. About Devon. Who has never, in the years I've known him, given a compliment he didn't mean. Who is in fact slightly stingy with praise, which is part of why I like him.

So I'm standing at the coffee machine cross-examining a sentence that was offered to me as a gift. Looking for the receipt. Looking for the catch.

I didn't tell Sam about it. That's the thing I want to write down. Sam asked how the day was and I said "good, fine, normal," and we made dinner. I didn't say "Devon said something nice about the doc." Which would have been a perfectly normal thing to mention. People mention nice things from work. It's not bragging to tell your partner that a coworker said your work helped them. But I didn't, and I didn't because — I think — saying it out loud would have made it real, and if it were real I'd have to do something with it, and I didn't know what.

Compare this to the sentence I did say out loud on Wednesday, the Mei one. The bitter joke walked right out of my mouth without permission. The kind thing from Devon, I had to actively suppress. There's a budget here, apparently. A budget for what counts as sayable about myself at the kitchen table. The harsh stuff is free. The good stuff costs something I don't have.

What I actually did with the compliment, in the end, is what I always do. I filed it somewhere I can't easily find. If you asked me in three weeks whether anyone has said anything nice about my work recently I would say "hmm, not really, I don't think so," and I would mean it, and I would be wrong. Devon's sentence will not be available to me. Whereas the imagined sentence where Mei revises her opinion of me downward — that one I can quote from memory and I haven't even heard it yet.

I want to be careful here because I can feel myself trying to wrap this up neatly. To turn it into a lesson. *You should let compliments land.* *You should tell Sam when nice things happen.* That's the shape my brain wants to put on this and I don't quite trust the shape. It's too clean. It's the same voice that runs the meetings, trying to manage the entry the way it manages a doc.

The truer thing is just: Devon said something kind, and I handed it back, and then I spent the afternoon checking it for poison instead of enjoying it, and then I didn't tell the person I live with about it because I didn't want to take up the space. And none of those moves felt like decisions. They felt like the floor of the room.

I did, before I closed my laptop for the weekend, send Devon a slack saying *btw really glad the doc was useful, let me know if you want to jam on the next phase.* Which was — I think — me trying to give him something back. Trying to make the transaction even. I couldn't just receive it. I had to mail him a small package in return.

Sam is putting on a record in the other room. I can hear them humming. I'm going to stop writing now and go in there.

I still haven't called Riley.

entries.prompt: The last two entries have both been about work and the strange way you handle being seen as competent — let's go somewhere else. Tell me about the last time you and Sam disagreed about something small. What did the disagreement actually feel like from the inside, and how did it end?
entries.content:
Sunday morning, the question of whether to go to the thing at Jess and Theo's. They'd invited us a week ago — brunch-ish, outdoor, bring nothing or bring a pastry. Sam wanted to go. I had said yes when the text came in, in the automatic way I say yes, and then by Saturday night I was already constructing the architecture of not going.

It started in bed. Sam was reading and I was on my phone and I said, very lightly, "what time is the Jess thing tomorrow." Sam said, "eleven, I think. Why." And I said, "no reason, just trying to plan." Which was a lie. The reason was that I was already mapping out how to get out of it. If it started at eleven, and we got there at eleven-fifteen, and stayed an appropriate ninety minutes, we'd be home by one, and then the afternoon would be — and somewhere in that math I was looking for the seam where I could say *actually you go, I'll catch up with them next time.*

I didn't say that. I said, "I might be a little tired, we'll see how I feel in the morning." Which is the sentence I deploy when I'm trying to leave myself an exit without committing to using it. Sam, who has known me for seven years, put their book down.

"Do you not want to go?"

"No, I want to go."

"Because we don't have to go."

"I want to go."

"You're doing the thing where you're laying groundwork."

This is where it tipped. Because Sam was right, and being seen that clearly at ten-thirty on a Saturday night made something flare up in my chest that I would describe, if I'm being honest, as *how dare you.* Not at the level of the actual situation — at some other level, some much older level. The feeling was: *you are not allowed to know me that well. You are not allowed to name the move while I'm making it.* And underneath that, almost immediately, a softer and more humiliating feeling, which was: *please don't take this away from me, I need the option to bail.*

What I said was, "I'm not laying groundwork, I just don't know how I'll feel tomorrow."

What Sam said was, "okay," in the tone that means *I have decided not to push this but I am not convinced.*

And then we lay there for probably four minutes not reading, not talking, the lamp still on, and I could feel the disagreement humming in the space between us like a refrigerator. It wasn't even a disagreement about the brunch. The brunch was fine. I like Jess and Theo. I would, if transported there by magic, have a perfectly good time. The disagreement was about the move. Sam had named the move and I was furious at being named and also relieved and also embarrassed and also defensive and the whole stack was just — there, in the bed, while we both pretended to be settling in for sleep.

I want to describe what it felt like from the inside because it surprised me. It didn't feel like an adult disagreement. It felt like being twelve and having my mom say *you don't actually want to go to that sleepover, do you,* and being unable to tell whether she was reading me or planting the idea. That kind of feeling. A feeling where the ground underneath my preferences becomes uncertain and I can't tell which is mine — the wanting to go, or the wanting to bail, or the wanting to be the kind of person who goes. All three were live. None of them felt like the real one.

After a few minutes I said, into the ceiling, "I think I'm scared it'll be tiring and then Monday will be hard."

Sam said, "okay. That's a real thing. We could go for an hour."

I said, "yeah. An hour is good."

And that was it. The disagreement ended. Not resolved exactly — more like it deflated. Sam turned off the lamp. I lay there for a while longer with the residue, which was: gratitude that they hadn't let me get away with the sentence about being tired, plus a small hot coal of resentment that they'd seen through it, plus a quieter thing underneath both, which was something like — *it is so much work to be a person who says yes and means it, and I am tired in a way that brunch is not the cause of.*

We went. We stayed an hour and twenty minutes. I had a good time. Theo made a joke that genuinely made me laugh. On the walk home Sam said, "glad we went?" and I said "yeah, really glad," and meant it, and Sam squeezed my hand and didn't say *told you so,* which is one of the small daily mercies of living with them.

The thing I keep turning over is the four minutes of silence with the lamp on. That was the actual disagreement. Not the words. The words were almost nothing. The disagreement was me wanting to be allowed to escape via a socially acceptable fog, and Sam refusing to pretend the fog was real. And me not knowing, in that moment, whether I wanted them to keep refusing or to let me have the fog just this once.

I think I wanted them to keep refusing. I think that's why I married — well, why I'm with — someone who does that. But wanting it and liking it in the moment are different things.

Riley would understand this exact feeling. Riley used to do this with me in college, the gentle calling-out. I really do need to call.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of yes you give without meaning it — the Jess-and-Theo yes, the handing-credit-back-to-Devon yes. Tell me about a yes you gave this week to yourself: a promise, a plan, a small commitment you made in your own head. What's happened to it since?
entries.content:
Tuesday morning, on the walk to get coffee, I told myself I was going to read for thirty minutes before bed every night this week. Not as a resolution — I don't do those — but as a small clean sentence I said in my head with some warmth. *Thirty minutes. The novel on the nightstand. No phone in the bed.* I remember where I was when I said it: crossing at the light by the bakery, the one with the green awning, watching a man walk a dog that was too small for the leash he had. I felt good. The air was good. The promise felt like a present I was giving myself.

It is now Thursday night and I have read for zero minutes.

What's happened in the interim: Monday I was already in bed, Tuesday I'd had the thought on the walk so it didn't count yet, Wednesday I scrolled until eleven-forty and then thought *too late to start, the whole point was thirty minutes, fragments don't count,* and put the phone down and slept badly. Tonight, now, the book is two feet from me on the nightstand and I am at the kitchen table writing this instead, which I want to note is not nothing but is also not the thing I promised.

The interesting part isn't that I broke the promise. I break this kind of promise all the time. The interesting part is the specific shape of how I broke it, which is: I never even got to the moment of refusing. There was no moment where I said *no, not tonight.* There was just a series of evenings in which the promise quietly was not present. It wasn't on the schedule. It wasn't on a list. It existed only in my head, in the warm sentence at the crosswalk, and the warm sentence apparently does not survive contact with 10pm.

I notice that if Mei had said *can you read this thirty-page doc by Friday,* I'd have read it. Twice. I'd have made notes. I'd have done it on the train if I had to. The promise to her would have had structure — a deadline, a recipient, a consequence. The promise to me had none of those, because apparently I don't qualify for structure. I get the warm sentence at the crosswalk and that's the budget.

This is the part where the voice that runs the meetings wants to step in and design a system. *Put it on the calendar. Set a phone-down alarm. Leave the book open to the page.* I can feel the doc forming. I could write the doc. I have, in fact, written the doc, in various forms, several times in my life. The doc works for about nine days.

I don't think the doc is the answer. I think the thing I want to look at is the warm sentence itself, and what I was actually promising.

Because I don't really care about reading thirty minutes a night as a metric. What I care about — what was underneath the warm sentence at the crosswalk — was something more like: *I want to be a person whose evenings have a shape that is mine.* The phone scrolling isn't restful. I know it isn't. I get up from it more tired than I sat down. The reading would not just be reading. It would be evidence, to me, that I am still the person who reads. That there is still an interior life here that I am tending to and not just renting out to whatever my thumb finds.

And I think I didn't do it because doing it would mean confronting how far away from that person I've drifted. Thirty pages of a novel would be fine. Thirty pages of a novel after a year of not really reading would also be a small grief. *Oh. I used to be able to sit with a book for an hour without checking anything. I used to mark passages. When did that stop.* The promise was warm at the crosswalk because it lived in the future. In the bed at 10pm it would have to become present, and present means measured against what is, and what is is harder to look at.

So instead I scroll, which has the great advantage of producing no evidence of anything.

I want to be careful not to make this too tidy. The truth is also just: I'm tired in the evenings, and reading is hard when you're tired, and a phone is easy. That's a real thing too. Not every failure is psychological. Sometimes the couch is comfortable.

But I notice the same shape from the other entries. The yes to Jess and Theo that I was already planning to escape. The handing-back to Devon. And now this — a yes to myself given freely on a Tuesday morning that I never actually intended to keep, or that I intended to keep in the way you intend to send a thank-you card, which is to say warmly and indefinitely and not really. The yes was the whole event. The follow-through was always going to be optional.

What I want to try, and I'm aware of how small this is: tomorrow night I'm going to read for ten minutes. Not thirty. Ten. The thirty was the warm sentence. The ten is what fits in the actual bed at the actual hour with the actual amount of energy I actually have. If I do ten I will have done something. If I aim for thirty I will do zero, and then I will add it to the running ledger of my failures, which is already long enough that one more entry won't even register, which is part of why I keep aiming for thirty.

Ten minutes. The book is right there. I can see it from here.

I am also, while I'm being honest, going to text Riley tomorrow. Not call — text. The call is the thirty minutes. The text is the ten. Something like *hey, I miss you, can we find a time.* That much I can do.

Sam just came in and asked if I'm coming to bed. I said in a minute. I'm going to go now.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt genuinely angry — not annoyed, not frustrated, but actually angry — and what did you do with it? Walk me through where you were, what set it off, and what you did with the feeling once it arrived.
entries.content:
Last Thursday, on the phone with my mom. Around six-thirty, I was still at my desk, hadn't started dinner, and she called the way she calls — cheerful opener, weather report, a thing about the neighbor's dog, and then the question. *Have you been getting outside? You sounded tired last week.*

I said yes, I'd been walking in the mornings. Which is true. And she said, in the voice she uses, *good, because you know how you get when you don't.*

That was the sentence. *How you get.*

Something went hot in my chest very fast. Not the slow build of being annoyed, where you can feel it accumulating and you have time to manage it. This was instant. Like a match. I was standing at my desk holding the phone and the room got a little smaller and I could feel my jaw locking and a sentence forming in my mouth that I have never said to my mother, which was something like *you don't get to diagnose me from eight hundred miles away based on a phone call where I sounded tired because I was tired.*

I didn't say it. Of course I didn't say it. What I said was, "yeah, I know, I've been good about it." In a tone that was maybe two degrees cooler than the rest of the call and that she may or may not have registered.

But the anger didn't go away when the sentence got swallowed. It just relocated. I could feel it sitting under my sternum for the rest of the conversation, which went on for another twenty minutes, while she told me about a thing at church and a thing with my aunt and a thing about a recipe. I made the right noises. I laughed in the right places. I was furious the entire time and she did not know.

After we hung up I stood at the desk for a minute and then I — and this is the part I want to write down honestly — I went and reorganized the silverware drawer. Not as a coping move. I didn't decide to do it. I walked into the kitchen with the intention of making dinner and I opened the silverware drawer and I noticed that the spoons were mixed in with the forks in the front compartment and I just started taking everything out and putting it back in correctly. I did this for maybe six minutes. I was breathing in a way that was not quite normal. When Sam came home I had cleaned the drawer and the counter and started the rice but had not yet decided what we were eating, which Sam picked up on immediately because the rice was on but nothing else was happening and I was wiping a spot on the counter that did not need to be wiped.

Sam said, "everything okay?"

I said, "my mom called."

Sam said, "ah."

And then I did something I don't always do, which was: I told them. I said, "she did the *how you get* thing. About not going outside. And I am so much angrier than the sentence warrants and I don't know what to do with it."

Sam, chopping something, said, "it's not about the sentence."

Which I knew. But hearing them say it let me put the rag down.

What I want to try to describe is what the anger actually was, because in the moment it felt like it was about the comment. *How you get* is patronizing, it implies a documented pattern of falling apart that does not match the actual record of my adult life, it puts her in the position of expert on a person she sees three times a year. All of that is real. But the heat of the thing was disproportionate to the offense. People say worse to me at work and I feel nothing. My mom says *how you get* and I want to throw the phone.

I think what I was actually angry about — and I'm working this out as I write — is that she's not entirely wrong. There is a version of me that gets quiet and walks less and eats badly and doesn't call anyone, and she has seen it, and she has a name for it, and the name is *how you get.* And the anger isn't that she's wrong. The anger is that she's allowed to know that, from a phone call, after I've spent the entire week constructing a version of myself for everyone else that does not show that seam at all, and she pulls it out in the first three minutes like it's nothing. Like *of course* that's still in there. Like she can see it through the performance.

It's the same shape as Sam in the bed last Saturday. *You're laying groundwork.* The fury at being seen accurately. Except with my mom there's an extra layer, which is that being seen accurately by her also means being seen as the kid I was, the one who needed to be told to go outside, and I have spent — god, twenty years? — trying to not be that kid anymore, and one sentence on a Thursday call puts me right back in the kitchen of the house I grew up in, fifteen years old, being told what I am.

What I did with the feeling, in the end: I told Sam, we ate the rice with some chicken, I went on a walk after dinner because I'll be damned if I was going to skip the walk after that, and I did not call her back to say any of this. I'm not going to. The conversation that would clear it is not a conversation she and I know how to have. That's a real thing too, and I'm not going to pretend it isn't by drafting a brave email I won't send.

But I noticed the anger. I let it be anger and not "frustration." That's something. Small. Ten minutes, not thirty.

I read for twelve minutes last night, by the way. The book is good.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you laughed at something and meant it — not the polite laugh, the real one? Where were you, who were you with, and what was funny about it?
entries.content:
Saturday afternoon at the grocery store, of all places. We were in the cereal aisle, which is not where things happen, and Sam picked up a box of something called Oat Clusters and read the front of it out loud in the voice of a local news anchor. *"Oat Clusters: now with more cluster."* And I lost it. Not a polite laugh. A real one, the kind that comes up from somewhere lower than your throat, where you have to put your hand on the cart to steady yourself and you can feel people looking at you and you don't care.

It wasn't even that funny. Written down it's nothing. *Now with more cluster.* I'm aware. But Sam delivered it with this absolute commitment, the slight chin tuck of a man reporting a four-alarm fire, and the box was so smug about its clusters, and I had been in a mood all morning that I hadn't quite identified, and the laugh came out and kept coming. Sam started laughing because I was laughing. We stood there in the cereal aisle for maybe forty seconds, both of us going, neither of us able to stop, and then every time we almost recovered Sam would say *cluster* again, in the news voice, and we'd start over.

There was a woman with a kid in her cart who gave us a look that was half *please* and half *I remember.* I wiped my eyes with the back of my wrist and we moved on to pasta.

What I want to write down is what the laugh felt like, because it surprised me how rare it was. Not rare like I never laugh — I laugh all day. I laugh in meetings. I laugh at things Devon says in slack. I laugh when the barista says something dry. But those laughs are — I don't know how to put this without sounding precious about it — those laughs are produced. They come out of the part of me that runs the meetings. They are accurate to the situation. They land on time. They are the right length.

The cereal aisle laugh was not produced. It bypassed something. It came up from underneath the part of me that monitors whether a laugh is appropriate and arrived in the body before I'd cleared it. I noticed afterward that my shoulders were lower. Like the laugh had unscrewed something. I have read this somewhere, that real laughter does something to your nervous system, and I'm sure that's true, but in the moment I just felt — porous. Easy. Like the version of me that exists in the cereal aisle with Sam is a version with thinner walls.

I think this is partly why I love them. Not the joke — the joke was nothing. The fact that Sam will, with no audience, in the middle of an errand, fully commit to a bit about cereal. There's no payoff for them. They're not performing for me. They just thought it would be funny, and it was, and they didn't need anything back from me except the laugh, which was free.

Compare this to — and I'm aware I do this, compare every nice thing to its joyless cousin — compare this to most of my laughing at work, which is in service of something. Smoothing a meeting. Acknowledging that someone made a joke. Letting Mei know I got her dry comment. Those laughs are functional. They are part of the labor. The cereal laugh was not labor. The cereal laugh was a small refund on the labor.

I want to be careful here because I can feel myself trying to make this into a thesis about something. *You should have more cereal-aisle laughs.* The voice that runs the meetings would love to take this and turn it into a project. *Schedule joy.* God. No.

What I notice instead is just: it happened, and it was a Saturday, and I wasn't trying. I was in a mood I hadn't named. I was probably going to be a little short with Sam at some point that day because the mood was looking for a place to land. And then the cereal box happened and the mood evaporated. Not because we addressed it. Because the laugh did something the mood couldn't survive.

Riley used to make me laugh like that. That's the thing I sat with later, on the walk home, both of us carrying bags. Riley had this specific gift for the absurd straight-faced thing, and we used to do it back and forth for entire meals, building something stupid into something elaborate. I have not laughed like that with Riley in — I genuinely don't know. Two years? Three? Because we don't see each other. Because the texts we send are *miss you* and *let's catch up soon* and not the actual stupid thing about the cereal. The medium doesn't carry it. You can't text a news-anchor voice. Or you can, but it dies on arrival.

I texted Riley on Friday, by the way. The way I said I would. I said *hey, I miss you, can we find a time.* Riley wrote back within an hour. *yes please. next sunday afternoon?* And I said yes. So that's a real thing on the calendar now, a phone call with a date attached, not the warm sentence at the crosswalk.

I wonder if I'll be able to laugh like that on the phone with Riley. Or if too much time has passed and we'll do the catch-up version of laughing, the polite version, the one that's actually a sigh. I hope not. I think Riley is still Riley. I think I'm still, somewhere, the person who could do the bit back.

The other thing about the cereal laugh: I told Sam, walking home, that I'd really needed that. And Sam said, "I know." Not in a smug way. Just — they'd known. They'd seen the mood I hadn't named, and they'd done the news voice on purpose, or half on purpose, the way you toss a ball gently to someone who looks like they could use catching something.

That's the part I want to remember. Not the joke. The fact that someone in my life is paying attention closely enough to make me laugh on purpose, and I didn't even know I was being taken care of until afterward.

I've been re-reading that paragraph and almost cutting it because it sounds sentimental. I'm going to leave it.

entries.prompt: Describe something small you did this week that nobody else would have noticed — a choice you made when no one was watching, a kindness or a meanness you did in private, a way you spent five minutes that you wouldn't have spent that way if someone could see. What was it, and what was going on in your head while you did it?
entries.content:
Wednesday, mid-afternoon, between meetings. I had about eleven minutes before the next call. I closed the laptop, which is unusual — I normally just mute and stand up — and I went and sat on the floor of the bedroom with my back against the side of the bed.

That's it. That's the thing nobody saw. I sat on the floor for eight or nine minutes.

I didn't put on music. I didn't do a meditation app. I didn't even really close my eyes. I sat on the rug with my back against the bedframe and looked at the baseboard across the room and the little pile of Sam's running shoes by the closet, and I breathed, and the breathing was — it was the first time all day I had noticed that I was breathing. Up to that point I'd been on calls and in slack and in a doc, and my breath had been doing whatever it does when I'm not paying attention to it, which apparently is shallow and high in the chest.

What was going on in my head: at first, a list. *You have eleven minutes. You could clear the inbox. You could pee. You could refill the water. You could glance at the doc one more time before the call.* The list ran for maybe forty seconds and then thinned out, and I didn't replace it with anything. I just kept sitting. The radiator clicked. There was a car alarm a few streets over. The light on the floor moved very slightly.

And then a thought came up that I want to write down accurately because it surprised me. The thought was: *nobody knows where I am right now.*

Not in a dramatic way. In a small, almost amused way. Sam was at work. Mei was on a call. The slack would assume I was at my desk because the green dot was on. The calendar showed me as available. The whole apparatus of being-tracked that I live inside, voluntarily, all day, did not know that I was sitting on the bedroom floor doing nothing. There was a small pocket of unobserved life in the middle of a Wednesday and I was inside it.

That was the feeling. *Unobserved.* And what surprised me was how much I liked it. Not relief exactly. More like — the air was different in there. The version of me that sits on the floor when nobody knows is a different person than the version of me that sits at the desk where the green dot is on. The floor version doesn't have to perform competence for anyone, including herself. The floor version is just a body against a bedframe, breathing.

I noticed I was a little embarrassed by how good it felt. Like, *really? eight minutes on the floor and you're having a moment about it?* The voice that runs the meetings was definitely there, slightly bemused, slightly impatient. *This is what we're doing now? We're a person who sits on the floor?* But the voice was quieter than usual, because the voice also requires an audience to do its job, and on the floor with the door closed there wasn't one.

I want to be honest about what else was in my head, because it wasn't all peaceful. At some point in the middle of it I thought: *I should be doing this on purpose. I should make this a practice.* And I felt the doc starting to form again — the way it formed for the reading promise, the *put it on the calendar, set a timer, do this every day at 2pm.* I could feel the part of me that wants to convert the accidental thing into a system, because systems can be measured, and measured things can be done correctly, and correctly is the only mode I really trust.

I noticed it and I did not pick it up. I just kept sitting. That, more than the sitting itself, is the thing I want to remember. I had a small good thing and I did not immediately try to professionalize it. I let it stay accidental. I let it be a Wednesday afternoon on the floor and not the beginning of a regimen.

At some point I checked the clock on my phone — three minutes left. I got up. My legs were a little stiff. I went and rejoined the call and was pleasant and on-time and nobody on the call could have known that two minutes earlier I had been on the floor doing nothing. I got through the call fine. I think I was actually a little sharper than usual, but I might be inventing that retroactively to justify the eight minutes.

I didn't tell Sam about it. Not because it was a secret. Because saying it would have been like saying *I read for ten minutes* — true, but also a way of cashing it in for credit, and I didn't want to cash it in. I wanted to just have it. A small private thing that happened on a Wednesday and that I did not need anyone to know about for it to count.

I am writing about it now, which I notice is its own kind of cashing in. The journal is also an audience. But the journal is a softer audience than Sam at the dinner table or Mei in the meeting, and I think I can let it know without losing the thing.

What was going on in my head while I did it, finally, I think, was: *oh. This is also available. This whole register of being a person — the not-doing register, the unobserved register, the floor register — this is here. It has always been here. You can step into it for eight minutes between calls and step back out and nobody has to know.*

I don't know what to do with that yet. Maybe nothing. Maybe just write it down and let Wednesday be Wednesday.

The book is at fourteen minutes now, last night. Riley call is Sunday. I am, in small accidental ways, becoming slightly more porous. The cereal aisle. The floor. I think those are the same thing.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you wanted this week — not needed, not should-have-wanted, but actually wanted. Big or small. What was it, and what did you do about it?
entries.content:
Thursday afternoon I wanted, very badly, to leave work at four and go to the bookstore.

Not for any reason. Not because I needed a book. Not because I had a gift to buy. The wanting arrived around two-thirty in the middle of a meeting that was going long, and it was very specific. I wanted to be in the used bookstore on the avenue, the one with the narrow aisles and the cat, and I wanted to spend an hour there with no agenda. I wanted to pick up books and put them down. I wanted to read the first page of something and decide against it. I wanted to be a person whose Thursday afternoon contained a bookstore.

The wanting was not vague. That's what surprised me. Usually when I notice a desire it comes through a layer of fog — *maybe I'd like, hmm, something* — and by the time I've identified it, it's already been processed by the part of me that decides whether wanting it is reasonable. This one came in clear. I was looking at a slide deck and the bookstore arrived fully formed, like a postcard someone had slid under the door of my attention. The cat. The aisle. The hour.

What I did about it: I stayed in the meeting. The meeting ended at three-fifteen. I had a slack from Mei about a thing for Friday. I answered the slack. I had another call at three-thirty that I could have moved — it was internal, low stakes, two people who would have said *sure, no problem* — and I did not move it. I took the call. The call ended at four-twenty. By then the bookstore was still open but the wanting had — not gone away. Gotten quieter. Become theoretical. The bookstore at four-thirty was not the same bookstore as the bookstore at four. The four o'clock bookstore had a particular quality of light and emptiness that I had wanted specifically, and the four-thirty bookstore was just a store I could go to.

I went anyway. I want to write that down because part of me wants to make this a story about not going, about how I let the wanting die in committee, and the truer thing is that I went. I closed the laptop at four-twenty-five and put on my coat and walked the eleven blocks and was in the bookstore by quarter to five. I stayed until they started turning lights off in the back, which was about six. I bought a paperback of essays I'd never heard of because the first paragraph was good. I petted the cat, who tolerated it. I felt — fine. Pleased. A little tired. The wanting had been mostly satisfied.

But I want to be honest about the gap. Between two-thirty and four-twenty-five was almost two hours, and in those two hours I had multiple small chances to honor the wanting more directly, and I did not take them. I could have moved the three-thirty call. I could have left at four. I could have, even, if I'd been a different person, declined the meeting that ran long, which I had no real reason to be in past two. There were several seams. I did not use any of them. I used the seam that opened by itself at four-twenty-five, the one that didn't require me to actively choose the bookstore over anything else.

This is the thing I want to look at. I got the bookstore. I did not get the bookstore by taking it. I got the bookstore by waiting until the bookstore was the path of least resistance. Which means I did not actually act on the wanting — I let the wanting survive long enough that the world delivered it to me without my having to spend any social capital on it. *I left work because I was done with work,* not *I left work because I wanted to be in the bookstore.* Those are different sentences and I told myself the first one walking down the avenue.

Why does this matter. I think it matters because the wanting was clear, and I treated it as if it were not clear. I treated it as a preference to be weighed against other preferences instead of a thing I was allowed to act on. Two hours of meetings I could have moved, and the wanting just sat in the corner waiting to be picked up when nobody would notice. Like the floor on Wednesday. Like the ten minutes of reading. The pattern is: I get the good thing only when I can get it without anyone seeing me choose it.

If Mei had wanted me to go to the bookstore at four — if it had been an errand for her — I would have left at three-fifty. Easily. Cheerfully. With a calendar block.

I notice I keep coming back to this. The promise to myself doesn't have structure because I don't qualify for structure. The wanting doesn't get to override the schedule because the wanting is mine, and mine doesn't outrank Mei's, doesn't outrank the optional internal call, doesn't outrank the slack that could have waited until Friday morning.

I'm not going to make this into a project. I can feel the doc forming again — *honor your wants, leave at four when you want to leave at four* — and I'm going to let the doc not get written. What I want to write down instead is just: I wanted something specific on Thursday afternoon, I almost let the wanting expire, and then I caught it at the last available moment and got most of what I'd asked for. Not because I was brave. Because the calendar happened to clear.

The essays I bought are good. I read four pages last night, which I'm counting as my ten minutes plus extra. Sam asked what I'd bought and I showed them the book and they said, "oh, that looks like you," in a voice that was warm and accurate, and I let it land. I didn't hand it back. I just said thanks.

Sunday is Riley. Three days away. I am, slightly, looking forward to it. That's also a wanting. I'm writing it down before I can talk myself into being neutral about it.

entries.prompt: Think about someone in your life you haven't given much thought to lately — not estranged, just out of frame. Who came to mind, and what's the last real moment you had with them?
entries.content:
The person who came to mind was my dad.

Which surprised me, because I talk to him every week. He's on every call with my mom — he picks up the other line about ten minutes in, says hi, asks one or two questions, says he loves me, hangs up. He is not out of frame in any practical sense. He's in the frame for fifteen minutes a week, reliably.

But when I sat with the prompt, I realized I haven't had a real moment with him in — I genuinely had to think about it — maybe two years? Maybe more. The calls don't count. The calls are choreography. He asks about work and I give him the version. He tells me about a project in the garage. We say I love you. That's the whole shape, every Sunday. It's affectionate and it's nothing.

The mom call I wrote about last week — the *how you get* call — I didn't even mention that he was on it. He was. He picked up around minute twelve, said the thing about a hardware store, said he was glad I'd been walking, and got off. He was completely peripheral to the experience. He has been completely peripheral to most of the experiences I have had on the phone with my parents for years, and I have not, until tonight, found that strange.

The last real moment. Let me actually try to find it.

I think it was the summer before last. Sam and I drove out to visit, three days, and on the second afternoon my mom went to a thing at her sister's and it was just me and my dad in the house. I was in the kitchen making a sandwich and he came in and stood in the doorway and asked me if I was happy at my job. Just like that. Not the version of the question my mom asks, which is *are you taking care of yourself,* coded and full of trapdoors. He asked the actual question. *Are you happy there.*

And I said, "I think so. Mostly. It's a lot."

And he said, "yeah. It always is." And then, after a pause where he was looking at the kitchen window and not at me, he said, "I never really figured out the happy part. I figured out the doing-it part."

I remember I stopped making the sandwich. I had the knife in the mayo and I just stopped. Because my dad does not say things like that. My dad says things like *that's a good question* and *we'll see* and *your mother would know.* He is a person who has, my entire life, deferred the inner-life conversation to my mother, who is herself not great at the inner-life conversation, so it has mostly just not happened. And here he was, in the doorway in the middle of a Tuesday, telling me he never figured out the happy part.

I didn't know what to do with it. I said something like, "yeah, I think a lot of people are in the doing-it part." Which is true and also was a sentence designed to take the temperature back down. I gave him the safe version. He nodded. He went back to the garage. We did not bring it up again. The next morning he made pancakes and asked if I wanted blueberries and we were back in the regular weather.

I have thought about that moment maybe four times since it happened. I didn't tell Sam about it. I didn't tell Riley about it. I have not, until now, written it down. It has been sitting somewhere unlabeled for almost two years.

What strikes me, sitting here, is that my dad gave me something that afternoon. He told me a real thing about his life. And I — politely, with the same reflex that handed Devon's compliment back — I deflected it. I gave him a sociological observation about *a lot of people* instead of meeting him where he was. He had opened a door and I had said *yes, doors* and walked past it.

I don't think he was upset. I think he was probably relieved, on some level — he'd said the thing, the air had been moved, and he could go back to the garage. But I also think there was a version of that afternoon where I put the knife down all the way and said, *what do you mean, the happy part,* and we stood in that kitchen for another twenty minutes and I learned something about my father that I do not currently know. And I didn't take it. I was thirty and I still didn't know how to receive that from him, so I didn't.

The thing I want to write down is that I am, apparently, my father's daughter in a specific way I had not clocked. The doing-it part, not the happy part. The version of life where you are functional and reliable and present at the kitchen table and you do not, generally, say the thing underneath. He has been doing this for sixty-plus years. I have been doing it for, what, twenty? I'm on the same track. The performance I've been worrying about with Mei is a smaller version of the performance my dad has apparently been running his whole life, and the cost of it — the *I never really figured out the happy part* cost — is something he was trying, in his way, to warn me about. And I missed it. Or I half-caught it and put it down.

I don't know what to do with this. I'm not going to call him. The call would be weird. He would not know what to do with me bringing it up two years later, and the truth is I'm not even sure I want to bring it up — I want to know him better, but the conversation that would do that is not one we have a script for, and inventing the script over the phone on a Tuesday is more than either of us can carry.

But I want to remember that he said it. *I figured out the doing-it part.* I want to keep that sentence somewhere I can find it. He gave it to me on purpose. He picked the afternoon when my mom was out. He was, in his quiet way, trying.

I might ask him, on Sunday's call, what he's working on in the garage. Not as a code for the bigger conversation. Just because I'd like to actually hear about it. That's the ten-minute version. That's what fits.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel like you were performing being yourself — going through the motions of a version of you that other people recognize, while something underneath wasn't really showing up? Tell me where you were and what the gap felt like.
entries.content:
Friday night, drinks with Mei and Devon and two people from another team I don't know well. A bar with too many lights. We were celebrating the launch — sort of. The launch had been fine. The drinks were the punctuation.

I was on. I want to say that clearly because I think it's easy to write *I was performing* and have it sound like I was suffering. I wasn't suffering. I was good at it. I told a story about a thing that happened earlier in the week and I told it well, with the right beats, and Devon laughed at the part where he was supposed to laugh and Mei did her dry one-line follow-up that meant she'd liked it, and one of the people from the other team said *oh my god, that's so you,* even though she has known me for maybe six weeks. I felt the sentence land. *That's so you.* Like a stamp on a passport for a country I was visiting.

The gap was small at first. I noticed it around the second drink. We were talking about weekend plans and I said something light about reading, about the essays I'd bought, and the woman from the other team said *oh, what kind of essays,* and I — I watched myself do it — I made it into a joke. Something about how I'd bought them to seem like the kind of person who reads essays, ha ha, who has time, ha ha. The table laughed. I laughed. And the actual book, the one I'd read four real pages of and liked, the one Sam had said *that looks like you* about — that book was not in the conversation. A cartoon of the book was in the conversation. A bit. A version of buying-a-book that was easier to share than the actual buying of the actual book.

I could feel it happening and I did not stop it. That's the part. It wasn't unconscious. The gap was small and lit up from the inside. I knew the real answer — *they're essays about cities, I'm only four pages in, the first paragraph was about a bridge and I liked it* — and I gave the joke instead, because the joke was the right size for the table, and the real answer would have made everyone do a tiny adjustment, the kind of adjustment where the conversation has to slow down for a second to receive an actual sentence. I didn't want to make the table adjust. I wanted to keep the rhythm.

So the bit went in and the book stayed home.

This kept happening. I noticed it after that and then I couldn't unnotice it. Mei asked how the weekend looked and I said *oh, low key, probably nothing,* and the truth was I had a phone call with Riley scheduled on Sunday afternoon that I had been thinking about for four days, that I was nervous about, that mattered. I did not say *I'm calling my best friend from college, I haven't really talked to her in two years, I'm a little scared of it.* I said *low key, probably nothing.* Mei said *nice, sounds restorative,* and I said *yeah, exactly,* and we moved on.

Every one of these was — fine. Socially fine. Nobody was being deceived in any meaningful way. This is what people do at bars on Friday nights. You give the table the version of your life it can metabolize between sips. I know this. I've always known this. What was new on Friday was that I could feel, very precisely, the thing I was trading away. Each small joke-version was a small not-saying of the real thing. And the real things, taken together, were most of what was actually happening to me this week. The book. Riley. The eight minutes on the bedroom floor. The wanting at the bookstore. None of it was at the table. None of it was sayable at the table. The version of me at the table was the version that does the bit about Oat Clusters but cleaned up, professionalized, the cereal-aisle laugh repurposed as material.

The gap felt like — I want to get this right — it felt like being slightly ahead of myself. Like the words were arriving a half-second before I'd checked them, because I'd gotten so good at the shape of what the table wanted that I didn't need to check anymore. Autopilot in a body. The body laughing on time. The body picking up the drink on the beat. The body saying *that's so true* to something that was only sort of true. And underneath, very quietly, a small voice going, *where are you, where are you, where are you.*

I was the last one at the table to leave, because I'd been so on that I didn't notice I was tired until everyone else stood up. Walked home. Cold air helped. Sam was already in bed reading. They asked how it was and I said *fun, exhausting,* and they said *the good kind?* and I thought about it for a second and said, *I don't know yet.*

That was the truest sentence I'd said in five hours.

Sam closed the book and looked at me. They didn't push. They said, *come here,* and I lay down with my coat still on for a minute before I got up to actually undress. I think I was, in some small way, trying to find my way back into the body that had been at the bar. Trying to let the version of me that had been performing get smaller again so the rest could come back into the room.

I want to be careful not to make the bar into the villain. The bar was fine. Mei and Devon are people I like. The performing wasn't a betrayal of them — it was just the medium. You don't bring the bookstore-want to a bar. You don't bring your dad's *I never figured out the happy part* to a table of five. I know this.

But I noticed that I came home with less of myself than I left with. And I noticed that most Fridays end like this, and I have just been calling it *tired,* the way my mom would call it *how you get,* and not looking at what specifically had been spent.

Sunday is Riley. I am going to try, on the phone, to say one real thing without the joke around it. Just one. The book, maybe. Or that I missed her. Something that doesn't need the table's permission.

entries.prompt: Last Sunday you wrote about wanting the bookstore and not going. Setting that specific afternoon aside — when's another time recently you've watched yourself talk yourself out of something you actually wanted? Walk me through the negotiation as it happened.
entries.content:
Tuesday evening, around six. I had finished work and Sam was going to be late — some thing with their team, dinner not until eight-thirty. I had two and a half hours and nothing on the calendar.

The wanting that arrived was: I want to go to that wine bar on the corner by myself and sit at the bar and eat the little plate of olives and the bread thing and read the essays for an hour.

It came in clear, the way the bookstore came in clear. I could see it. The bar is small, six stools, dim in a way that's flattering to a paperback. They have that bread with the whipped butter. I'd been there twice with Sam and once with Devon from work and I had thought, both times with Sam, *I would like to come here alone sometime.* Not because I wanted to be away from Sam. Because I wanted to be the kind of person who sits at a bar alone with a book on a Tuesday.

I was already putting on my coat when the negotiation started.

It started as logistics, which is how it always starts. *What time will Sam be home, are you sure it's eight-thirty, what if they get out early and come home and you're not here.* I checked the text. Eight-thirty was firm. Fine. Logistics passed.

Then: *do you actually want the olives or do you want the idea of the olives.* This is a move my brain does that I want to write down because it's slick. It reframes the wanting as a possibly-fake wanting, a wanting that's really about an image of myself, and once it's about an image then having it is vain and not having it is humble and not-having becomes the more honorable choice. I noticed this happening and I almost let it work. *You don't really want the olives. You want to look like a person who wants the olives. Just stay home and read the essays on the couch, it's the same book either way.*

Except it isn't the same book either way. I know it isn't. The four pages I read on the couch don't feel the way I imagine the pages at the bar would feel. The location is part of what I was wanting. The wanting was specific.

Then: *it's expensive.* This one is true and not the real reason. A glass of wine and a small plate at that bar is maybe twenty-eight dollars with tip. I spend that on lunch with Mei without thinking. But twenty-eight dollars on myself, alone, on a Tuesday, with no occasion, felt — I want to be precise — felt like more than twenty-eight dollars. Felt like a declaration. *I am the kind of person who spends twenty-eight dollars on a Tuesday evening to sit alone and read.* The math wasn't the problem. The declaration was the problem.

Then: *what if someone you know is there.* This was the one that landed. This was the one I let win. I imagined walking in and seeing Devon at a table with someone, or worse, Mei, and having to do the small wave, the *oh hi, just grabbing a quick thing,* the explanation that I am here alone with a book on a Tuesday. The explanation I would not actually have to give, because Devon wouldn't ask and Mei would just nod, but the explanation I would feel myself preemptively constructing the whole walk over. The whole point of the bar alone was to be unobserved, the way the floor on Wednesday was unobserved, and the possibility of being observed — even by people who would not care — corroded the wanting in advance.

I took my coat off.

I made a quesadilla. I read on the couch for about twenty minutes and then I scrolled until Sam came home.

What I want to write down is the specific move where I lost. It wasn't the logistics. It wasn't the money. It was the *what if someone sees you.* And what's strange is that I would not have been doing anything embarrassing. A woman at a bar with a book is not embarrassing. I have, in fact, admired women at bars with books. I have thought, *good for her, I should do that.* The issue is not what other people would see. The issue is that I would have been visible while doing something for myself with no cover story. No work reason, no Sam, no errand. Just me, choosing to take an hour and twenty-eight dollars and put them on myself in public.

The bookstore on Thursday I got because the bookstore opened up a seam where it could look like I was just leaving work. Nobody had to read the bookstore as a choice. The wine bar would have required me to walk in and announce, by the act of sitting down, *I am here on purpose, I picked this, I am worth this hour.* And I could not, on a Tuesday at six, make that announcement.

I want to note something, because I think it's the actual thing. I have been writing, for ten entries now, about the gap between what I want and what I do, and the shape that keeps appearing is: I can have the good thing if it arrives accidentally, or in private, or under cover of something else. The floor between calls. The bookstore at the end of the day. The reading in bed where nobody can see the page count. What I have not yet been able to do is walk into a public room and sit down and be a person who chose this. That's the move I keep not making. The wine bar was that move and I didn't make it.

I'm not going to make it next Tuesday either, probably. I want to be honest about that. But I'm writing it down so that when I do, eventually, walk in and order the olives, I will know what I did.

Riley call is in five days. That's also a public-ish room, in its way. A room with one other person in it. I'm going to try to walk into that one on purpose.

entries.prompt: There's a sentence you said this week that other people received as a joke, or as charm, or as competence — but you knew, while you were saying it, that it wasn't quite what you meant. What was the sentence, who were you with, and what would the truer version have sounded like?
entries.content:
Monday afternoon, in the one-on-one with Mei. We were doing the standing weekly thing, half about the work and half about — well, ostensibly about me, my development, whatever language we use for it. She asked, near the end, a version of the question she asks every few months: *anything you want to be doing more of? Less of?* She means it. She is genuinely asking. She has the calendar block held open for the answer.

What I said was, "honestly, I think things are good — I'd love to keep getting more of the cross-team stuff, I feel like that's where I'm growing the most."

She nodded, said something appreciative, wrote it down. We wrapped a few minutes early. Pleasant meeting. Productive, even. I went and made tea.

The sentence was received as competence. As ambition appropriately expressed. As a person who knows what she wants and is positioned to ask for it. It was a good answer — I want to be clear about that. It wasn't a lie. The cross-team work is interesting. I do want more of it. If you'd transcribed the sentence and shown it to anyone, they would have said, *yes, that's a normal thing to say in a one-on-one, well done.*

But while I was saying it, I knew it wasn't what I meant. I knew it the way you know a key isn't quite the right one even before you turn it — there's a small wrongness in the fit, a half-millimeter off, and you push anyway because you don't have a better key on you.

What I actually wanted to say, if I'd had a sentence ready, was something like: *I'd love to be less afraid of you.*

I'm writing that down and looking at it and feeling slightly sick. But it's the truer version. The thing I want more of in our working relationship is not cross-team scope. The thing I want more of is the ability to be in a room with Mei without running the whole second conversation underneath the first one. The *she's recalibrating, she's revising, she's noticing.* The whole apparatus I described in entry one. I want that apparatus to be quieter. I want to be able to take a redirect on a doc without spending the next forty minutes constructing a story about how it means I'm slipping. I want — and this is the part I really cannot say in a one-on-one — I want her to like me less specifically. I want the stakes of her opinion to be lower in my body. The cross-team work is a fine answer because the cross-team work is about scope and competence and growth, all of which are sayable. *I want our relationship to be less terrifying to me* is not sayable. It would land in the room like something dead.

So I gave the sayable answer and she wrote it down and the meeting ended.

What I keep turning over is that the answer I gave will probably also produce real outcomes. She'll route some cross-team thing my way. I'll do it well. It will go on a list of things I have done. By the metrics anyone would use, including me, this was a successful one-on-one in which I advocated appropriately for my own development. The fact that I was, the entire time, performing competence at the person I am most afraid of performing competence in front of — that part doesn't show up anywhere. That part is just the weather inside.

I notice the shape, and I want to write it down because I think it's the same shape as Friday at the bar. The version of the sentence that fits the room is the version that goes. The version that doesn't fit stays in. Mei's office is a room. The bar is a room. The kitchen with Sam, sometimes, is a room, though the rooms with Sam have thinner walls. The dad-in-the-doorway moment was a room I didn't know how to be in. The cereal aisle, somehow, was not a room. That's the difference. The cereal aisle had no walls. The one-on-one has walls made of HR-acceptable language and the standard shape of the development conversation, and inside those walls only certain sentences can survive.

I am not going to walk into Mei's office next Monday and say *I'd love to be less afraid of you.* I want to be clear with myself that I am not building toward that. That sentence is not for her. It's not even, really, a sentence that wants to be said out loud. It's a sentence that wants to be known. By me. The work is in the knowing, not in the saying.

What I am going to try, and I am aware of how small this is, is to notice the gap when it happens. Just the noticing. *That was the sayable sentence. The truer one was —.* I don't have to deliver the truer one. I just want to know it's there. I want to stop letting the sayable sentence become, retroactively, the thing I actually meant, because that's the move that makes me feel, at the end of a Friday, like I came home with less of myself. The sayable sentence consumes the truer one if I'm not paying attention. It eats it and walks away wearing its clothes.

Mei's note is in the doc. *Wants more cross-team.* Fine. True enough. Let it be true enough.

The truer sentence is in this entry. That's where it lives. That's enough for a Monday.

Riley call is Sunday. Four days. I keep mentioning it like I'm winding myself up to it. I think I am.

entries.prompt: The last few entries have all circled the same shape — a moment where you watched yourself perform competence or ease and felt the gap. Let's go somewhere else. Write about a recent stretch of time you spent alone where no performance was required. What did you actually do with the hours, and what was the quality of being in your own company?
entries.content:
Saturday morning, Sam was at the long run with their group, the one that takes up most of the morning and ends with bagels. Out the door at seven-thirty. Back, realistically, by eleven-thirty or so. Four hours of empty apartment.

I want to write down what I actually did, which is less and more than I expected.

I made coffee the slow way. Not as a ritual — I just didn't have anywhere to be, so the time the water took was fine. I stood at the counter while it dripped and looked out the window at the building across the street, where a woman on the third floor was watering something on her sill. I watched her for probably a minute. She did not know I was watching. I was not, in any sense, doing anything. I was just a person at a window with a kettle.

I drank the coffee on the couch and did not open my phone for — I checked, later, by looking at the screen time — about fifty minutes. This is the longest stretch of phone-free morning I have had in I don't know how long. It wasn't a decision. I just didn't pick it up. The book was on the arm of the couch from the night before and I picked that up instead, and I read for a real stretch, maybe forty pages, which is more than I have read at one sitting in a year. The essays are about cities. The one I read this morning was about a bridge in a city I have never been to, and I thought, while reading it, *I would like to go there sometime,* and the wanting was small and unargued-with. It just sat next to me on the couch.

I did the dishes from last night. I had been planning to leave them for Sam, which is a thing we do without keeping score, and instead I just did them, not virtuously, just because the kitchen was right there and my hands wanted something. I put a record on. I do not, normally, put records on by myself. Records are a Sam thing — they're the one who knows what's in the stack and what mood goes with what. I picked one I knew Sam liked and put it on and didn't think about whether it was the right choice.

Around ten I got dressed and went for a walk. No destination. I want to be precise about this because it surprised me — I went out the door without a coffee shop or an errand or a route. I just walked. I went down the street I usually walk down to get to the train and then turned the other way, into a part of the neighborhood I don't usually have a reason to be in. I walked past a school and a church and a park I had not been in before, and I sat on a bench in the park for maybe fifteen minutes and watched a man teach a small child to throw a ball. The child was bad at it. The man was patient. I was not part of this scene in any way. I was just present for it. The light was good.

What I want to write about is the quality of those four hours, because I keep almost reaching for a word and the word keeps being wrong. *Peaceful* is wrong. *Restorative* is wrong — that's the word I gave Mei at drinks and I knew it was wrong then. *Quiet* is closer but still not right. The closest word I can find is *unhurried.* Nothing in the four hours was hurried. The coffee took as long as it took. The reading went on until I was done with the essay. The walk lasted until I came home. I was not, at any point, ahead of myself.

The strangest part, and this is what I want to remember: I was not performing for me, either. That's the part I didn't know was possible. I have been writing for weeks about performing for Mei and the table and Sam and even the journal, and I think I had assumed that the part of me that performs would just turn its attention to me when there was no one else to perform for. *Now we'll have a productive Saturday. Now we'll be the version of us that reads and walks and is well.* I was braced for that voice. It mostly didn't come. There was a small flicker around nine when I thought I should probably make a list of what to do with the morning, and I didn't make the list, and the flicker passed. After that it was just — being in the apartment. Being on the bench. Being a body moving through a Saturday at the speed it actually moved at.

I want to be honest that some of this is the prompt's fault. I knew I was going to write about the morning. I knew, somewhere in my head, that I was banking material. So in some small way the morning was already being narrated. I caught myself, on the bench, almost composing this paragraph. I let it pass. The morning continued without me writing it down.

Sam came home at eleven-forty with bagels. I had not done much. I had not, by any external measure, used the four hours. The kitchen was a little cleaner. I had read forty pages. I had walked a couple of miles. The biggest thing I had done was nothing visible — I had been alone with myself for four hours and not, for once, made a project out of it. Not the regimen project, not the productivity project, not the be-a-better-person project. Just been around.

Sam handed me the bag and said, "good morning?" and I said, "yeah, really good," and meant it and didn't elaborate. They didn't push. We ate the bagels at the counter.

I think the floor on Wednesday and the cereal aisle and this morning are all the same thing. They are stretches where I am not a person being graded. Where the whole apparatus of being-watched, by Mei or by the table or by the version of me that runs the meetings, just — is not deployed. And in those stretches I am, apparently, fine. Not transformed. Not enlightened. Just fine. A person at a kettle. A person on a bench.

Riley call is tomorrow. I notice I'm less wound up about it than I was on Tuesday. Maybe the morning helped. Maybe four unhurried hours is what I had been needing, and not a script.

entries.prompt: Who's someone you've been a little short with lately — clipped tone, fast reply, less patience than they deserved? What was the moment, and what do you think was actually going on for you underneath it?
entries.content:
Wednesday evening, Sam, in the kitchen, about something so small I'm almost embarrassed to write it down. They asked me whether I'd taken the recycling out, and I said, "I said I would, didn't I," in a tone I would not use on a stranger. The tone was — clipped is the right word. Two degrees colder than the question warranted. Sam blinked, said "okay, just asking," and went into the other room.

The recycling, for the record, had not been taken out. I had said, that morning, that I'd do it before they got home. I hadn't. The reminder was reasonable. The reminder was, in fact, the gentlest possible version of the reminder — a question, not a statement, framed as if maybe I'd already done it.

I stood at the sink for a minute after they left the room and did the small inventory of what had just happened. The tone had not been about the recycling. The tone had not even been about Sam. The tone had been waiting in my throat for hours and the recycling was just where it landed.

What had actually been happening that day: Mei had sent a slack at four-fifteen asking if I could "take a quick look" at a deck before tomorrow's meeting, and I had said yes within ninety seconds, the way I always say yes within ninety seconds, and the deck had turned out to be forty slides, and I had spent from four-thirty to six-forty-five going through it and writing comments and being, in the comments, the careful diplomatic version of myself who softens every redirect with *one thought here* and *might be worth considering.* By the time I closed the laptop my jaw was tight and I had not eaten and I had not, obviously, taken the recycling out. I had also not said, at any point in the slack exchange, *actually four-fifteen is late, can this wait until morning,* which would have been a reasonable sentence and which I did not produce.

So by the time Sam asked about the recycling I had a small hot pile of unsaid sentences in me, none of them addressed to Sam, all of them about Mei and the deck and the reflex that makes me say yes in ninety seconds. And the pile had to go somewhere. Sam walked into the kitchen and asked a small fair question and the pile picked them.

This is the move I want to write down because I think it's the one I do most. The clipped tone with Sam is not, almost ever, about Sam. It's the residue of a yes I gave someone else that I couldn't take back. Mei doesn't get the cold tone. Mei gets the *of course, happy to take a look.* Sam, who would have been entirely understanding if I had said *I'm wrecked, I had to do a thing for Mei, can you grab the recycling,* gets the snap. The person who would absorb the truth gracefully gets the version of me that has no truth left to spend, because I spent it all maintaining the surface for the person who wouldn't absorb it.

I want to be careful not to make Mei the villain here. Mei did not make me say yes in ninety seconds. Mei sent a slack. The slack had a question mark in it. I produced the yes. The yes is mine. What's mine is the part where I converted Mei's request into an obligation that could not be pushed back on, and then carried the cost of it into a room with someone I love, and handed them the bill.

I went into the living room after a few minutes. Sam was on the couch with their phone. I said, "hey. Sorry. That was sharper than it needed to be." Sam looked up and said, "yeah, I noticed." Not punishing. Just accurate. I said, "Mei sent me a thing at four-fifteen and I've been doing it until just now and I didn't eat and I'm — I think I'm angry at myself and I put it on you." Sam said, "yeah, that tracks." And then, after a second, "you could have told me you were doing that. I would have made dinner."

Which is the thing. I could have told them. I didn't. Because telling them would have made the four-fifteen yes visible as a choice, and as long as it stayed invisible I could pretend it had been unavoidable, and unavoidable things don't require me to look at why I made them. The clipped tone is part of how the choice stays invisible. If I'm short with Sam about the recycling, then the story of the evening is that I'm tired and a little snappish, not that I said yes to something I should have pushed back on and then made my partner pay the interest.

I took the recycling out. It took ninety seconds. Sam reheated some pasta from the night before and I ate it standing at the counter and we watched something dumb. By nine the apartment was fine again. None of which is the point. The point is that there is a pattern where I am most short with the person I am safest with, on the evenings when I have been most accommodating to someone I am not safe with, and the unsafety with the second person gets paid for by the first.

I don't know what to do with this except notice it. The doc would say *push back on Mei's late slacks,* and the doc would not survive Friday afternoon. What I might be able to do, smaller, is notice the pile in my throat before it lands on Sam. Maybe just say, when I walk out of the office at six-forty-five, *I am carrying something, it is not yours.* Out loud, to Sam, before the recycling question can find me.

I'm going to try that. Not as a regimen. Just as a sentence I might have available.

The Riley call was good, by the way. I haven't written about it yet. I will.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a moment recently when you were genuinely surprised by something — not amused, not impressed, just caught off-guard. Where were you, what happened, and what was the half-second before you arranged your face into a reaction?
entries.content:
Tuesday morning, on the phone with my dad. Just him, not my mom — she was at a doctor's appointment, nothing serious, and he had picked up because he saw it was me on the screen and figured he'd answer.

We did the usual five minutes. Weather, the car, a thing about the gutters. I was making oatmeal one-handed and the call was sitting at the comfortable low frequency it always sits at, the one where I can be doing two other things and still be present enough.

And then, without any setup, he said, "I've been thinking about quitting the volunteer thing."
