The volunteer thing is a thing he has done for, I want to say, twelve years. It's the thing he does. It's the thing my mom describes him through when she's talking to her sister. It's on the bio of the church newsletter. He goes Tuesdays and Saturdays and has gone Tuesdays and Saturdays for as long as I have been an adult.

I want to write down the half-second, because that's what the prompt is asking, and the half-second is the part I'm trying to hold onto.

In the half-second I was — I had the wooden spoon in my hand and I had stopped stirring and I felt my face do nothing yet, because my face hadn't gotten the instruction. There was a small empty stretch where I had heard the sentence and not yet decided what to do with it. The sentence had landed and was just sitting there, and I was still on the spoon, and the oatmeal was bubbling slightly too hard on the back burner, and I was aware of all of that at once. Aware of the bubbling, aware of him on the line, aware that he had said something he did not normally say, aware that whatever I said next would be the thing he heard.

What was in the half-second, mostly, was: *oh. He's telling me something.*

Not the content. Not yet. Just the shape of it. The same shape as the kitchen doorway two summers ago. The *I never figured out the happy part* shape. He had picked the morning when my mom was at the doctor and he had picked the moment in the call after the gutters and he had said the thing without any wind-up at all, and I was — I was caught. Because I had not, on a Tuesday at eight-fifteen, with oatmeal on the stove, been ready for him to do this again. I had filed the kitchen-doorway moment as a one-time thing. A weather event. I had not understood that he might be a person who does this, occasionally, and that I might be the person he does it to.

The half-second was that recognition arriving without a sentence attached. *Oh. This is the thing he does. This is twice now.*

Then my face caught up. I said, "oh — really? what's making you think about it?" In a tone I want to describe carefully. It was a tone two clicks softer than my normal phone tone. Not the *that's so true* tone from the bar. Closer to the tone I use when Devon tells me something hard about his sister. I had, somehow, in the half-second, found the right tone. Or my mouth had. I don't know which of us did it.

He said, "ah, you know. The legs aren't great. And I don't know if the new guy who runs it likes how I do things. And I think I've been doing it because I've been doing it." Pause. "Your mother thinks I should keep going."

I turned the burner off. That was a thing I did consciously. I turned the burner off so that the oatmeal would stop being a reason to half-listen.

I said, "what do *you* think you should do."

He laughed, the small dry laugh he does, and said, "I think I might be done. I haven't told her that."

And then — and this is the part I want to remember — he changed the subject. He asked about the project I'd mentioned last week. He went back into the regular weather. He had said the thing, the air had been moved, and now he wanted to be somewhere else. I let him. I asked about the project the way you ask. We finished the call in the usual shape. I love you, love you too, talk Sunday.

I stood at the stove with the cold oatmeal and I thought, *he told me something he hasn't told my mom.*

That's the surprise. Not the volunteer thing. The fact that there is a category — apparently there is a category — of things my dad tells me and not her. I did not know this category existed. I had assumed, my whole adult life, that my mom was the channel and he was the appendix. The picking-up-the-other-line guy. And here he was, on a Tuesday, using me as the person he says the unsaid thing to. Picking the morning she wasn't there. Doing it on purpose.

I don't know what to do with it. I don't think I have to do anything with it. I think the work, this time, is just to not file it the way I filed the kitchen doorway. Not put it in the unlabeled drawer for two years. To know, on Sunday when he picks up the other line and says the thing about the hardware store, that there is a different version of him available, occasionally, under specific weather, and that I am — apparently — someone he will sometimes show it to.

I texted Sam from the kitchen. *My dad just told me something. Tell you tonight.* Sam wrote back, *ok. love you.*

I ate the cold oatmeal. It was fine.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel relief — that specific exhale when something you'd been bracing for turned out fine, or got cancelled, or just passed? Where were you when it landed, and what had you been carrying right before it?
entries.content:
Friday afternoon, four-twenty, the slack from Mei: *hey, gonna push our 1:1 to next week, drowning today, sorry.* Three lines. A small apologetic emoji at the end.

The exhale was so loud I actually heard it. I was at my desk and I made an audible sound, the kind of sound you make when you put down a heavy bag, and I looked around afterward like someone might have heard. The office was mostly empty. Nobody had heard.

What I had been carrying, right before it: the one-on-one was at four-thirty. I had been, since about lunchtime, pre-running it. Not consciously — I wasn't sitting there scripting — but the second conversation had been on, low frequency, all afternoon. *What is she going to ask. What's the version of the answer. Did she like the deck comments. Was the framing one in the third comment too sharp. Did I sound like I was hedging. Will she bring up the cross-team thing.* The whole apparatus from entry twelve, humming. I had been answering slacks and editing a doc and on a call about a roadmap, and the entire time, underneath, the one-on-one was costing me something. A small tax on every other thing.

And then it was gone. Reschuduled to Tuesday. Five days away. Tuesday, in the way the future is always slightly imaginary, was not real yet. The one-on-one had, for the next hundred and twenty hours, ceased to exist.

I want to write down what the relief actually felt like, in the body, because it told me something. The relief was bigger than the meeting deserved. The meeting was thirty minutes. The meeting was, statistically, going to be fine — they always are. Mei is, by every reasonable measure, kind to me in those meetings. She has never, in two years of weeklies, said anything cruel. The relief I felt at four-twenty was not proportional to escaping a thirty-minute weekly with a person who likes me. The relief was proportional to escaping something much bigger. Which means I had been carrying something much bigger.

That's the thing the exhale told me. The amount of dread that left my body when the slack came in was the amount of dread I had not, until that moment, known I was holding. I had been calling it *getting ready for the one-on-one.* It was actually *bracing.* I have been bracing every Friday from about noon onward for two years, and I have not, until the slack cancelled it, been able to feel the size of the bracing, because there was no version of the day where the bracing got to stop.

I sat there for a minute. I did not immediately fill the time with something. I did not, which is what I would normally do, slack Mei back with *no worries, totally, hope you get out from under it,* though I did, eventually, send that. First I just sat. The four-thirty block on my calendar said *Mei 1:1* in gray and I looked at it and the gray block did not make me clench. It was just a gray block. A meeting that wasn't happening.

What I noticed next was strange. I noticed I was a little disappointed.

Not about the meeting itself. About the relief. The relief was so good that I knew, immediately, I was going to have to give it back. Tuesday is five days. The bracing will start again, probably Monday afternoon. The exhale was a one-time refund, not a permanent change in pricing. And the part of me that registered this was almost — annoyed at the relief? For being temporary? For showing me what a non-braced state felt like, which I would now have to lose again?

This is the same shape as the floor between calls. The same shape as the bookstore. I get the good thing only briefly, only when something else clears it, and then I have to hand it back. I cannot, apparently, walk into a Friday and just decide not to brace. Mei has to cancel for the bracing to release. The release is contingent on her, the way the bookstore was contingent on the calendar opening up.

I closed the laptop at five. I did not stay to do the *quick thing* I would normally do at five on a Friday to feel like I was using the time properly. I just left. Walked home. The walk was — I want to say *light,* but it's closer to *level.* I was walking at the speed I was actually walking. Not ahead of myself. Not narrating. Just moving down the avenue with my bag on my shoulder. The Friday-evening light was doing the thing it does in late fall, where everything goes slightly gold for fifteen minutes and then it's over.

Sam was home. I came in and they were chopping something and I said, "Mei cancelled my one-on-one and I feel like a different species." Sam laughed and said, "good. Stay this species for a while." I sat at the counter and watched them chop. I did not pick up my phone. I drank a glass of water and watched the knife and thought about nothing in particular and the kitchen smelled like garlic.

Around seven I noticed the bracing had started to come back, very faintly. A small thread of *Tuesday, what about Tuesday.* I noticed it and I didn't pick it up, the way I didn't pick up the doc-shaped thought on the floor. It went away for a while. It will come back. That's fine. I'm not trying to fix it tonight. I'm trying, just, to remember what the exhale felt like, so that the next time I'm bracing on a Friday afternoon I have, somewhere, a sense memory of what I'm bracing against. What the size of it actually is. How loud the sound is when it leaves.

I think that's the thing. I have been writing for fifteen entries about a gap, a performance, a second conversation, a tax. Today I felt the tax stop being charged for ninety minutes and I made a sound. The sound is the data. The sound is what I want to remember.

Sam asked if I wanted wine. I said yes. I meant it.

entries.prompt: Think about a stranger or near-stranger you crossed paths with this week — a barista, someone on the train, a delivery person, a neighbor in the hallway. Pick one and tell me about the small exchange. What did you notice about them, and what did you imagine about their life after?
entries.content:
Thursday morning, the woman at the dry cleaner. I was picking up Sam's coat, the wool one with the belt that had come off in the pocket, and she was the one behind the counter — not the older man who's usually there, who I have a whole running thing with about the weather. She was younger than him. Maybe sixty. Glasses on a chain. She had not looked up when I came in because she was doing something with the ticket machine, pressing buttons that weren't responding, and I stood there for maybe forty-five seconds while she pressed.

When she looked up, her face did a thing. Not an apology, exactly. A small acknowledgment that I had been waiting and that the waiting had been because of the machine and not because of her. She said, "this thing." Two words. She said them with a small flick of her hand at the machine. And I laughed, a real one, because the *this thing* was so exactly the right amount of language for the situation. Not a customer-service apology. Not an explanation. Just *this thing.* A person and a broken object and another person who was going to have to wait, and all three of us in the room.

I gave her the ticket. She found the coat. She had to walk to the back, and she walked the way people walk when their hip is doing something — not limping, but planning each step a little. I noticed it because she was being deliberate about not letting me notice it. She came back with the coat in the long plastic bag and rang me up, and when I tapped my card the reader made the sound it makes and she said, "good," like the reader had also been giving her trouble that morning and this one had cooperated.

I said, "have a good one," in the automatic way, and she said, "you too, dear," and the *dear* was — I want to write this down accurately because it caught me. It was not a customer-service *dear.* It was the kind of *dear* my mom's friends used to say to me when I was twelve and being polite at a church function. A *dear* with no transaction in it. A *dear* that was, mostly, just affection at a low temperature offered to someone she didn't know.

I walked out with the coat over my arm and I was thinking about her by the time I hit the corner.

Here is what I imagined. I imagined that the older man is her husband, and that he was at home with something — a doctor's appointment, a bad night's sleep, the kind of thing that means you don't go in on Thursday — and she was covering. I imagined she usually does the books and the alterations in the back and doesn't deal with the counter, which is why the ticket machine was being a thing for her, because she doesn't use it every day. I imagined the shop has been theirs for thirty years. I imagined the *dear* is something she says without thinking, the way some people say *honey* or *love,* and that her grown kids tease her about it.

None of this is information. I want to be clear with myself. I made all of it up between her counter and the corner. It is possible she's not married. It is possible she owns the shop alone. It is possible she's the older man's sister, or his employee, or someone he hired last month. The story I built was mostly furniture for a feeling I couldn't name yet, which was that I had liked her. That the forty-five seconds at the broken machine and the *this thing* and the *dear* had added up to a small warm thing in a Thursday I had not expected to have a small warm thing in.

I think what I actually noticed about her, under the made-up biography, was that she was not performing. The *this thing* was not customer service. The *dear* was not customer service. She was at the counter on a morning she maybe hadn't planned to be, dealing with a machine that was not cooperating, and she was being — a person. Slightly tired. Slightly amused. Not constructing anything.

And I noticed, walking with the coat, that I had been a person back. I had not done my counter voice. I had laughed the real laugh at *this thing.* I had said *have a good one* in the regular way and not the bright way. The exchange had happened at whatever temperature she had set, which was low and warm, and I had matched it without having to think about it.

I have been writing for weeks about rooms with walls and rooms without. The dry cleaner at nine-fifteen on a Thursday was a room without. I think it was without because she didn't put any walls up. She came to the counter with no walls and the room took the shape of that. I did not have to do anything to find the unwalled register; she handed it to me by being in it first.

I want to remember her. Not as a lesson — I don't want to turn her into *be more like the dry cleaner woman.* Just as a person who was at a counter on a Thursday and gave me, for ninety seconds, a temperature I don't usually get to be at with strangers. I imagined her, later, eating a sandwich at the back table where the sewing machine is. I imagined her telling the older man, that night, about the machine. I imagined her not thinking about me at all, which is fine, which is correct, which is part of why the ninety seconds worked.

The coat is in the closet. Sam hasn't worn it yet. When they do, I might tell them about her. Or I might not. It might be one of the things I just keep.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been meaning to say to someone — not a confrontation, just a thing you've been carrying that hasn't made it out of your mouth yet? Who's it for, and what stops it each time you get close to saying it?
entries.content:
The thing I've been meaning to say is to Riley, and it's that the call three weeks ago was hard for me in a way I haven't told her.

The call itself was good. I want to say that first, because I keep almost letting the harder thing eat the good thing, and the good thing was real. We talked for an hour and twenty minutes. She picked up on the second ring. We did the catch-up at the start, the jobs and the apartments and the partners, and then somewhere around minute thirty we slid into the actual register, the one we used to live in, where she said something dry about her sister and I laughed the real laugh, and I felt — porous. Cereal-aisle porous. With her voice, on the phone, on a Sunday afternoon, after two years.

What I haven't told her is that I cried for about ten minutes after we hung up.

Not from sadness, exactly. From — I don't have a clean word for it. The closest I can get is that the call had shown me how much of my life I have been living without that register available, and the showing was its own small grief. We had been in the unwalled room for an hour and twenty minutes and then the call ended and I was back in the apartment and Sam was at the store and I sat on the couch with the phone face-down on my knee and I cried in the way you cry when you didn't know you'd been holding something. Quiet. Brief. Then done.

The thing I want to say to Riley is some version of: *that call mattered to me more than I let on, and I don't want us to go two years again, and I'm scared we will.*

What stops it, each time I get close.

The first thing that stops it is that we had a perfectly good call, and saying *that call mattered* would, I'm afraid, retroactively make the call itself feel like it had stakes it didn't appear to have. We were light. We were funny. We did the bit about her coworker's haircut for ten minutes. If I now write to her and say *I cried after,* she will have to go back through the call and reread it as a more serious event than it was, and I am protective of how easy it felt. I don't want to make it heavier than it was by reporting on its weight.

The second thing that stops it is that I am afraid she didn't cry. I am afraid the call was, for her, a nice catch-up with an old friend, the kind of thing she does with several people, and that the size of what it was for me would land in her inbox as disproportionate. *Oh. Maya is — a lot. Maya is bringing a lot to this.* I would rather under-claim the call than risk her finding out it had been load-bearing for me in a way it wasn't for her. The asymmetry is the part I can't bear. If the call meant the same thing to both of us, the sentence would be easy. If it meant more to me, the sentence is a confession.

The third thing — and this is the one I want to write down because it's the truest — is that saying it would make the gap between us a real thing we'd both have to look at. Right now the gap is a soft fact. We don't talk much, we love each other, life is busy, that's how friendships are at thirty-one. If I say *I don't want us to go two years again,* I am naming the two years as a thing that happened, a thing that cost me something, a thing I am asking her to help me not repeat. And then she has to either agree, which would be tender and would also obligate her, or demur, which would tell me something about where I actually rank, which I am not sure I want to know.

I notice this is the same shape as the Mei sentence. *I'd love to be less afraid of you.* The truer version sits in the throat and stays there because saying it would change the room. With Mei I decided the saying wasn't the point — the knowing was. I want to think the same thing here. I want to write *the knowing is enough,* and put the journal down, and let Riley remain a person I exchange occasional warm texts with.

But I don't think it is enough this time. Because Mei is a working relationship that will end, eventually, when one of us changes jobs, and the second conversation under it will end with it. Riley is — Riley is supposed to be the person I get to be unwalled with for the rest of my life. If I keep the truer sentence in my throat with her, the throat is where the friendship lives, and the friendship gets smaller and smaller until it fits in a text that says *miss you, soon.*

So the sentence I have been almost-writing, for three weeks, in drafts I don't send, is something like: *that call was a big deal for me. I didn't realize how much I'd been missing you until I heard your voice. Can we put another one on the calendar before this one gets too far away.*

Three sentences. None of them are confrontations. None of them ask her for anything she can't give. I can write them in under a minute. I have written them, in various forms, and not sent them, four times.

I think what I'm going to do, tonight, is send the third one. Just the calendar one. *Can we put another one on. Before this one gets too far away.* The first two sentences I'll keep for now. The calendar is the ten-minute version. The *I cried after* is the thirty.

If she says yes to a date, the rest can come on the call. If she says yes to a date and we get on the phone and the unwalled room opens up again, maybe I'll be able to say it inside the room, where it's warm, instead of mailing it to her in writing where it has to survive the trip alone.

I'm going to text her now. Before I lose the nerve I had at the start of this entry.

entries.prompt: Where's a place you go regularly — a coffee shop, a route home, a particular chair — that's started to feel different to you lately? Describe the last time you were there and what shifted, even slightly, about how it felt to be in it.
entries.content:
The chair in the corner of the living room, the one that used to be where I read before I stopped reading. Green, low arms, a small dent in the cushion exactly the shape of where I used to put the book against my thigh. I bought it five years ago at an estate sale and dragged it home in a borrowed car. For about a year after that, it was the chair. I read in it most evenings. Then I read in it sometimes. Then it became the chair where I dropped my bag when I came in.

For the last two years, functionally, it has been a coat rack. There is a hook by the door. The coats end up on the chair anyway. Sam's running jacket has lived on the back of it for, I want to say, six months. There has been a small pile of mail on the seat. A scarf I don't wear but haven't put away. A book I started in March and abandoned, page seventy-something, the spine cracked open so long it's gone soft.

I sat in it on Saturday afternoon. That's the thing I want to write down.

I had cleared it earlier in the week without making a project out of it — I'd come in with groceries, set the bags down, and instead of putting them on the chair like usual I'd noticed the chair was full of stuff that wasn't mine in the right way and I'd just — moved everything. Hung the running jacket. Recycled the mail. Put the scarf in the drawer. Took the abandoned book to the shelf. The chair was empty for the first time in I don't know how long. I went and put away the groceries and forgot about it.

Saturday I came back from a walk and the chair was still empty. The afternoon light was on the seat in the way I had forgotten it does — comes through the window over the radiator in a slant and lands on the green for about an hour between three and four. I had the new essays in my hand because I'd been reading them on the bench at the park. I sat down in the chair without thinking about it. I put my feet on the ottoman, which was also a thing I had not done in a long time, and the cushion did the small slow give it does, and I read for forty minutes.

What shifted — and this is the part I'm trying to find the right size for — is that the chair felt like mine again. Which sounds small. It is small. But I had not realized, until I was sitting in it, that I had been living for two years in an apartment where I did not have a chair. I had been reading on the couch, when I read at all, which is mostly Sam's spot — they're the one who reads on the couch, with their legs over the arm, the lamp at the right height for them. I had been borrowing their reading position. I had not noticed that I was borrowing it, because I had stopped reading enough for the borrowing to register as a problem.

The chair is mine. I bought it. I picked it. It is the right height for me and the wrong height for Sam, who is taller. Sitting in it on Saturday I remembered that I used to sit in it on purpose, and the remembering was — it was the same flavor as the *oh* in entry seven, on the bedroom floor. *Oh. This is also available. This has been available the whole time. You just stopped using it.*

I want to be careful, because I can feel the doc forming again. *Reclaim the chair. Read in the chair every night. Make the chair a practice.* I am not going to do that. The chair on Saturday worked because I sat in it without a plan and read until I stopped wanting to. If I make it a practice it will become a place I am supposed to be at a certain time, and the chair will become Mei and I will start bracing on the way to it.

What I think I'll do instead is just — not put the coats on it. That much I can manage. The coat hook by the door has been there the whole time. The chair becoming a coat rack was a slow accumulation, not a decision, and the un-accumulating can also be slow. One coat at a time, hung on the hook instead of the chair, and after a while the chair stays empty, and on the afternoons when the light is right and I happen to be home with a book I can sit in it without having to clear it first.

The other thing I noticed, and I want to write this down because it surprised me: while I was reading on Saturday, Sam came in from the kitchen and saw me in the chair and stopped for a second. They didn't say anything. They just — registered it. The way you register a thing you hadn't realized had been missing. Then they kept walking. Later, much later, after dinner, they said, "you were in your chair today." Not a question. An observation. I said, "yeah." And that was the whole exchange.

But I think Sam had been noticing the chair-as-coat-rack longer than I had. I think they had been watching me not have a chair for two years and had not known how to point at it. And then Saturday I was in it, and they registered, and they waited until evening to say it, and they said it in three words.

The chair is empty right now. I'm at the kitchen table writing this. I can see it from here. The light is gone — it's after nine — but the seat is clear and the cushion has the small dent in it. I think I'll go sit in it when I'm done. Not to read. Just to sit. To let the chair know I remember.

entries.prompt: Was there a compliment or piece of praise you got this week — at work, from a friend, in passing — that you deflected or downplayed? Tell me what was said, who said it, and what you did with it in the half-second after.
entries.content:
Tuesday, in the kitchen with Sam, after dinner. They were rinsing plates and I was wiping the counter and they said, without turning around, "you've seemed more like yourself lately."

That was the sentence. *More like yourself.*

The half-second after was a small disaster, internally. I want to write it down because it happened fast and I can still feel the shape of it.

First, before any thought, my hand kept wiping the counter. The wiping did not pause. The wiping was the cover, the way the laugh was the cover for the Mei sentence in entry one. As long as I was wiping, I did not have to fully be in the room receiving the sentence. The cloth was doing something with my body so the sentence could pass through without me having to catch it.

Second, a fast inventory. *More like yourself. What does that mean. What had I been before. Had I been visibly not like myself. For how long. Has Sam been watching me be not-myself this whole time and waiting. Is this a relief sentence — like, thank god you're back — or is it a noticing sentence, neutral. How long is the window they're talking about. A week? Two months? The whole last year?* All of that ran in maybe a second and a half and produced nothing useful, just a small fizz of self-consciousness.

Third, what came out of my mouth. I said, "ha — I think it's just that work has been quieter."

That was the deflection. Clean, fast, plausible. I handed the credit to the workload, the way I handed Devon's compliment to the thing he'd sent me last month. It wasn't even fully a lie — work has been a little quieter. But the sentence as I delivered it was designed to do one thing, which was to take Sam's observation about *me* and convert it into an observation about *circumstances.* Not *you've changed,* but *the weather around you has changed.* Circumstances I can let myself accept. A change in me, said out loud by the person who would know, I could not.

Sam, rinsing, said, "mm. Maybe," in the tone that means *I have decided not to push this but I am not convinced.* It is the same tone from the bed, the one before the brunch. I have a small catalog of Sam's tones now. That one is the *I'll let you have it for tonight* tone.

I kept wiping a counter that was, by then, dry.

What I want to write down is the part underneath. *More like yourself* implies they had a self of mine in mind that had been gone, and that they have been waiting to see it back. Which means Sam has, for some stretch of time I do not know the boundaries of, been watching me be a worse version of me, and not saying so, and now is saying so only because the worse version is receding. They didn't tell me when I was in it. They are telling me now, on the way out. That is — I want to be honest — that is a lot. The compliment is also an X-ray. *You were not okay for a while. I noticed. I waited.*

I don't know what I would have done with that information if Sam had given it to me three months ago. I don't know that there was a useful version of *you have not seemed like yourself lately* that I could have received in October, which is — when I think about it, when I let the timeline sit — probably about when it started, the *not like myself* stretch. Around when Mei gave me the framing redirect that became the kitchen sentence in entry one. Around when I stopped reading. The chair was already a coat rack. Sam was already noticing.

So when Sam said *more like yourself,* what they were also saying was *I have been holding the shape of you while you were away from it, and you have been coming back, and I wanted you to know I see you coming back.* And what I said was *work has been quieter.*

This is the move. I keep doing the move. Someone hands me a sentence about me and I hand them back a sentence about my circumstances, because circumstances are sayable and *you* is not. The chair, last weekend, Sam said *you were in your chair today,* and I said *yeah,* and that was fine because *yeah* is a small enough sentence that it didn't have to do work. But *you've seemed more like yourself lately* needs a bigger receipt than *yeah,* and I did not have one ready, and so I produced the workload sentence to keep the transaction small.

What the truer half-second-later sentence would have been, if I'd had it: *thanks. I think I have been. I didn't fully know I was gone.*

I am writing it down here because here is where the truer sentences live, apparently. The kitchen gets the workload version. The journal gets the *I didn't fully know I was gone.* That is not a triumph but it is, I think, slightly less of a loss than entry one was. In entry one the truer sentence was not even available to me on the page. It took the whole entry to find. Tonight I had it within ten minutes of the kitchen. The gap between the sayable and the true is closing a little, even if only on paper.

Later, while we were brushing teeth, I said, around the toothbrush, "hey — that was a nice thing you said earlier." Sam looked at me in the mirror. I said, "I don't really know what to do with it but I heard it." Sam said, "okay." Just okay. They reached over and squeezed my hip and went to bed.

That was as close as I got to not deflecting. It was about half a counter-wipe better than where I started.

Riley wrote back, by the way. We have a date on the calendar. Two Sundays from now. I'm leaving that here, at the bottom, the way I keep leaving things at the bottom — small facts I want the next entry to find.

entries.prompt: Money — when's the last time you spent some, or didn't spend some, in a way that felt a little revealing about you? Walk me through the moment at the register, or the moment you closed the tab.
entries.content:
Wednesday afternoon. The thing was a sweater on a website. Wool, a color between rust and brown that I have been wanting in my closet for a year without naming it. On sale. Not deeply on sale — twenty percent off, which is the kind of sale that's mostly a story the website is telling itself. The full price had been a hundred and eighty. The sale price was a hundred and forty-four. It was, by any reasonable metric for what I make, fine.

I had it in the cart for forty minutes.

I was supposed to be doing a doc. The tab was open in the corner and I kept clicking back to it. Looking at the model. Looking at the size guide. Reading the reviews, which were uniformly good in the slightly suspicious way reviews are when a brand has sent the sweater to the right people. *Runs true to size. Soft. I get compliments every time I wear it.* The compliments line is what made me hesitate, which is funny — I was reading a review about getting compliments and feeling, in real time, the small flinch of *not for me, then.*

What I did at minute forty-one was close the tab. Not buy it. Not save it for later. Close it.

The reasons I gave myself, in order: it was a lot for a sweater. The color might not work in person. I have other sweaters. I should wait and see if it goes on deeper sale. I should think about it overnight. The standard stack, deployed in standard order, none of them the real reason.

The real reason is that I could not, on a Wednesday afternoon, spend a hundred and forty-four dollars on a sweater I wanted just because I wanted it. The wanting wasn't enough of a reason. I needed an additional reason — an event, an old sweater that had finally died, a gift card, a thing I could point to that would explain the purchase to someone who wasn't asking. The wine bar was twenty-eight dollars and I could not walk in. The sweater was a hundred and forty-four and I could not click. The math is the same math. I do not, apparently, qualify for the unforced purchase.

I want to be precise about what I mean, because I don't want to make this into a story about thrift. I am not thrifty. I spend money on Sam without thinking. I bought Sam a jacket in October that cost more than this sweater and I did not hesitate for a second — I saw it, I thought *Sam would love that,* I bought it, I felt good about it for a week. I spend money on dinners with Mei and Devon. I spend money on the apartment. I spend money on books, at the bookstore, in person, where the spending feels like part of an experience and not like a declaration. The category I cannot spend in is: *I want this object, for myself, for no occasion, and the only justification is that I want it.* That category gets the forty minutes and the closed tab.

The thing I want to write down is what happened after I closed it. I felt — and this surprised me — a small mean satisfaction. *Good. You didn't.* As if not buying the sweater was a thing I had succeeded at. The voice that runs the meetings was pleased. *We are not the kind of person who buys a hundred-and-forty-four-dollar sweater on a Wednesday afternoon while supposedly working on a doc. We are disciplined. We have integrity.* And I let the voice be pleased for about fifteen seconds before I noticed what it was doing, which was converting a small joyless self-denial into a moral win. The same move as the wine bar — *you don't really want the olives* — but on the back end, after the fact, congratulating me for having listened.

The sweater would have been fine. I would have worn it. I would, probably, have gotten compliments, which I would have deflected, but the sweater itself would have done its job. The hundred and forty-four dollars would not have changed my life. The closed tab also did not change my life. Nothing changed. That's the thing. I had a small wanting and I administered a small punishment to it and went back to the doc and felt, for the rest of the afternoon, slightly diminished in a way I could not have pointed to if asked. Sam would have asked *how was your day* and I would have said *fine* and not mentioned the sweater, the way I didn't mention Devon's compliment, the way I didn't mention the bookstore until I'd already gone. The not-buying was unsayable in the same way the buying would have been. The whole transaction lived inside me with no witness.

I want to note one more thing. After I closed the tab I went and looked at my actual closet. I have, I counted, three sweaters in roughly the color range I was wanting. None of them are exactly the color. None of them is the one I had in the cart. But three sweaters is three sweaters, and the voice used them as evidence — *see, you didn't need it.* Which is true and is also not the question. *Need* was never the frame. The frame was *want,* and I had treated *want* as if it had to pass a *need* test, and of course it failed, because want and need are different tests and I had administered the wrong one on purpose.

I'm writing this and I can feel the doc forming. *Buy the sweater. Honor the want.* No. The doc would be wrong. The point isn't the sweater. The point is that I noticed the move, this time, while I was making it. I closed the tab and I knew, within about a minute, why I had closed it, and the knowing was different from the closing. The closing happened anyway. The knowing did not stop me. But the knowing was new.

The tab is closed. The sweater is presumably still on the website. I might, in two weeks, open it again and see. I might not. I am not going to make a rule about it. What I am going to do is write down, here, that on Wednesday afternoon I wanted a wool sweater the color of rust, and I could not let myself have it, and the reason was not the money.

Sam came home at seven. I did not mention the sweater. Some things, still, are easier to leave at the bottom of the entry than to bring into the kitchen.

entries.prompt: How did you sleep last night, and what was the first thing your mind went to when you woke up before you'd fully decided to be awake?
entries.content:
Slept fine until about four, then woke up the way I sometimes do — not from a dream, just up, eyes open in the dark with the heating making its small clicks. Sam was breathing the slow breath. The room was the regular room. I lay there for a few minutes and then got that feeling I get sometimes where I know I'm not going back under right away, and I considered the phone and didn't pick it up, which was a small mercy I want to credit myself with. Just lay there. Eventually went back. Woke up properly at six-forty when the alarm did its thing.

What my mind went to before I'd decided to be awake — and I want to write this down accurately because I caught it on the way up, in the small porous minute where you are not yet running the day — was the sweater.

Not in any organized way. Just: the color. The rust-brown. A small image of it, in my mind, draped over the back of the green chair. That was the picture. The sweater on the chair. Both of them empty in the apartment in the morning light.

And I lay there with the picture for a few seconds before I had any sentences about it, and what I noticed was that the picture was not making the case for buying the sweater. The picture was just — there. The sweater on the chair, both of them mine, both of them allowed. A small still life of a life I am not quite living yet. I was not, in the moment of seeing it, arguing for or against. I was just looking at it.

Then the day started. The voice that runs the meetings woke up about thirty seconds after I did. *Doc by eleven. Slack to Devon. Did you respond to the thing from the design team. The Tuesday one-on-one is tomorrow.* The picture got moved out of the way. Standard processing. I got up, made coffee, started.

But I want to stay with the picture for a minute because I think it's telling me something I would not have said to myself directly. The picture was not the sweater alone. It was the sweater on the chair. Two things I had wanted and not let myself have, sitting together in the morning light, in a composition that was — peaceful is the wrong word again — composed. Settled. The picture was of an apartment where the chair is empty and the sweater is here and I am the kind of person who has both of these things without it being a project.

I notice that I cannot, awake, picture the sweater on me. I can picture it on the chair. The chair is a safer place for the sweater than my body. On my body it would be — visible. Worn. People would see me in it and say things like *that color is great on you,* and I would have to do the deflection, the *oh thanks, it was on sale,* and the sweater would become the same thing every nice thing becomes, which is a small social transaction I have to manage. On the chair the sweater is just an object in a room. It belongs to nobody. It is just there, the color it is, in the light.

This is an interesting move. I want to write it down. I am, apparently, able to want things on the condition that I do not have to wear them in public. The sweater on the chair is allowed. The sweater on me is a problem. The chair empty is allowed. The chair with me in it being seen by Sam is — well, Sam saw me in it last Saturday and registered it and I survived. So that one I have done once. The sweater on the body I have not done.

I am writing this in the kitchen with my second coffee and I can see the chair from here. It is empty. Sam's running jacket is on the hook by the door, where it has been for twelve days now, since I started the small slow un-accumulating. The chair has stayed empty. I have sat in it twice this week, both times briefly, both times without a plan. The slow project is working in the way slow projects work, which is invisibly until you notice.

I think the four a.m. picture was the brain doing something the awake brain won't quite let it do. Putting two wantings together in a frame. Saying — without sentences, without arguments, without doc-shaped thoughts — *here is what would be nice. Here is what you almost let yourself have last week and didn't.* And then handing the picture up to the surface of consciousness in the small window before the day's machinery turned on, because that's the only window where the picture can pass through unedited.

I keep thinking about something I wrote weeks ago, that I get the good thing only when nobody is watching me choose it. The four a.m. version of me is, I think, a version of nobody-watching. There is no one to perform for at four a.m., not even me — the voice that runs the meetings is asleep. The picture got through because the editorial desk was unstaffed.

I am not going to buy the sweater today. I want to be honest about that. I have a doc by eleven and a slack from Mei from last night I haven't responded to and the day is going to do its day-shape and the sweater is not going to fit into it. But the tab is going to open again, probably tonight, after dinner, when the apartment is quiet. I am writing this down so that when I open the tab I will remember the picture. The sweater on the chair in the morning light. Both things mine. The four a.m. version, which is also me, having a clear small wish for both of them to be in the same room.

I don't have to act on it today. I just want to not pretend I didn't see it.

Sam is up. I can hear the shower. The day is starting. The chair is empty in the corner. The picture is in the journal now, where the truer things go.

entries.prompt: There's a thing you do where you wipe the counter, or laugh, or close the tab — some small physical move that lets a moment pass through you without fully landing. When's another time recently you caught yourself doing that? What were you covering, and what would it have been like to just stand still and take it?
entries.content:
Friday afternoon, the all-hands. The leadership update where someone announces something and then everyone reacts in the little squares. They announced that Jordan got promoted. Jordan, who I started at the same time as. Jordan, who I have been quietly tracking against for two and a half years without admitting that's what I'm doing.

The move I caught myself doing was the nod. The slow, warm, *of course* nod. I did it twice. Once when his name came up and the camera grid did its small ripple of clapping-hand emojis, and once when he came on to say a few words and thanked his manager. Both times my face did the nod and the small smile that goes with it, the one that says *yes, this is correct, I am pleased for this person, I am the kind of person who is pleased.* My camera was on. I knew my camera was on. The nod was for the grid.

What was actually happening in my chest during the nod was a small fast contraction, a *huh,* and then a hot wash that came up the back of my neck and settled behind my ears. Not rage. Smaller and meaner than rage. Something more like — *oh. So that's where the line was, and I was on the wrong side of it, and I didn't know there was a line.* And under that, almost immediately, the second wave: *of course Jordan. Jordan was always going to. You knew this.* And under that, a third thing I want to write down because it's the ugliest and the truest, which was: *what does Mei think about this. Is she watching my square right now to see how I take it.*

So I nodded. The nod was the cover. The nod was the wiped counter, the closed tab, the laugh. The nod let the contraction and the hot wash and the *what does Mei think* all happen inside a face that was, on the outside, doing congratulations. By the time the meeting moved on I had nodded twice and typed *congrats!!* in the chat with two exclamation points, and the small mean thing in my chest had been — not metabolized. Buried. Pressed down under the performance of being fine with it.

The all-hands ended. I closed the laptop and stood up and walked to the kitchen and made tea I didn't want, because the kettle was a thing to do with my hands, the way the cloth on the counter had been a thing to do with my hands when Sam said *more like yourself.* The kettle was the next move in the same family. A small physical errand to keep the body busy while a sentence I did not want to receive went past.

What I was covering, I think, is something I have not let myself look at directly in two and a half years. I have a quiet ranking I run, between me and Jordan and one or two other people at our level, and I have been losing it for a while, and I have been pretending I don't run the ranking because running it is embarrassing and losing it is worse. The nod was for two audiences. It was for the grid, yes. But it was also for me. The nod was telling me *you don't run a ranking, see? Look how warm the nod is. A person who ran a ranking would not nod like that. You are above this.* The nod was the move by which I lied to myself, in real time, about what I had just felt.

What it would have been like to stand still and take it. I have been turning this over since the prompt. I don't think standing still would have meant not nodding — the nod is, on some level, a social baseline, and I'm not interested in performing my interior on a forty-square grid. The standing still would have happened after. In the kitchen. Instead of the kettle.

It would have looked like this: I walk into the kitchen. I do not put the kettle on. I stand at the counter with my hands flat on it and I let the contraction be a contraction. I let the hot wash be hot. I let myself say, in my head, the sentence I do not want to say, which is *I wanted that and I did not get it and Jordan did and I am hurt and I am also a little ashamed of being hurt and both of those are happening and I am not going to pretend they aren't by making tea.* Maybe two minutes. Maybe four. Just standing at the counter with the thing.

I think what I am afraid of, with standing still, is that the thing would not be only about Jordan. The Jordan-shaped feeling is the surface. Underneath it is the longer thing — the *Mei is going to figure out I'm not as smart as she thought,* the second conversation, the bracing every Friday — and if I stood still long enough the surface feeling would drain into the underneath one, and I would be standing in the kitchen at four-forty on a Friday with the whole apparatus visible at once. The kettle prevents the drain. The kettle keeps the Jordan feeling Jordan-shaped and contained and survivable. Without the kettle I do not know what size the feeling would be.

I made the tea. I drank some of it. I closed slack and went for a walk before Sam got home, which I want to credit myself with — it was the same instinct as the kettle, a body errand, but at least it was a body errand that took me outside and not one that pretended to be productive. I walked for forty minutes. By the time I came back the contraction had gone down to a low hum.

I have not told Sam about Jordan. Not yet. I think I will, tonight. Not the whole apparatus. Just the sentence. *Jordan got promoted today and I felt worse about it than I want to admit.* That's the standing-still version, said out loud, eight hours late. Not the moment in the kitchen where I could have done it in real time. But closer than the nod. Closer than the kettle. The ten-minute version of standing still, which is what I can manage.

The tea is still on the counter, half-drunk, going cold.

entries.prompt: When you imagine the next year of your work — not the version you'd describe to someone at a dinner party, but the one you actually want — what's in it? Write the picture, even if it contradicts itself.
entries.content:
I'm at the kitchen table again, Sam at the gym, the chair empty across the room. I want to try this without editing as I go, which means I'm going to write some sentences I'd normally cross out.

The dinner-party version: I'd say something about wanting bigger scope, more cross-team work, eventually a manager track or a senior IC track, depending on where the org goes. I'd say it warmly. I'd mention Mei without saying her name. The person across the table would nod. Both of us would feel like we'd had a normal conversation about ambition. None of that is exactly a lie. It is also not what I would write down here if I were being honest, which is what the prompt is asking.

The actual picture, when I let myself look at it.

I want a year where Friday afternoons are not a tax. That's the first thing, and it's smaller than ambition and bigger than ambition at the same time. I want to walk into a one-on-one with Mei without the second conversation running underneath. I do not know how to engineer that. I don't think it's a thing I can put on a roadmap. But when I picture the next year, the feeling I keep reaching for is *unbraced.* Whatever the work is, I want to be unbraced inside it. That is the thing I would not say at the dinner party because *I want to feel less afraid* is not a career goal in any language the dinner party speaks.

I want to do work I'm proud of and not have the pride immediately convert into evidence I'll be found out. I want to send a doc and then close the laptop. Not refresh the slack. Not pre-run Mei's reaction. Send and close. I have done this maybe four times in two years. I want the next year to contain forty of them.

I want — and this one is harder to write — to be less interested in Jordan. I want the ranking to go quiet. Not because I've won it, which is the version the voice that runs the meetings would like, but because I've stopped running it. I do not know if that is possible inside the same org where Jordan and I sit at the same level minus one tier now. It might require me to leave, eventually, not because the job is wrong but because the proximity is wrong. I'm writing this down even though I don't believe it yet. I'm writing it down in case it's true.

I want one piece of work in the next year that is mine in a way that doesn't require Mei to bless it. Something I propose, or something I lead, or something I just — make, on the side, that gets used. The cross-team thing I told Mei I wanted is part of this, but the way I told her about it was the dinner-party version. The actual want is more like: I want to remember that I can build something without needing to check the temperature of the room every six minutes. I used to be able to do that. I made things in college, and in my first job, that I don't think I could make now, because making them required not asking permission, and I have, somewhere in the last few years, started asking.

I want to stop saying yes in ninety seconds. I have written this down before. I am writing it again because the picture of the next year has to include it or the rest of the picture is fiction. The cross-team work, the work I'm proud of, the unbraced Friday — none of those survive a year of ninety-second yeses. I do not know what the technique is. I know the technique is not a doc.

I want — this is the contradiction the prompt was warning me about — I want Mei to keep liking me. I notice this. I want to be less afraid of her opinion *and* I want her opinion to remain warm. Both at once. The mature version of the picture would be *I become indifferent to her opinion and that's what frees me,* but that isn't true to what I actually want. I want the relationship and I want the relationship to cost less. I'm not sure those are compatible. I'm leaving the contradiction in.

I want to be the person Devon describes when he describes me to someone outside the company. I have caught the edge of this a couple of times, in moments where Devon has clearly told someone something good about my work, and the someone treats me, when we meet, like I'm already a person worth taking seriously. I want to be that person to myself. Not as a brand. Just as a baseline. The way Devon, apparently, already sees me. I am writing this down and noticing that I am embarrassed by it.

I want a year in which I take a real vacation. Not the kind where I bring the laptop. I have not done this in three years. The picture includes a week somewhere I have not been, and the laptop in a drawer, and an out-of-office that does not say *will check periodically.*

I want, by the end of the year, to have had at least one conversation with Mei that is not the sayable version. I do not know what it would be about. Maybe nothing dramatic. Maybe just one sentence in a one-on-one where I say a true thing instead of the diplomatic one and the room does not end. Just to know it can be done.

I want to still be doing this — the writing — in a year. I notice I almost didn't put that in because writing isn't work. But it is the place where the truer sentences go, and the picture of the next year of work has to include the place where the work gets metabolized, or the work eats me.

Sam will be back in twenty minutes. The chair is empty. I have not bought the sweater. I am going to leave the picture here, contradictions and all, and not try to make it consistent before I close the notebook. The dinner-party version is consistent. The actual version is what I came here for.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you did for someone this week that you'd find embarrassing to be caught doing — not a grand gesture, just a small private effort on their behalf you wouldn't quite want them to see. What was it, and what were you thinking while you did it?
entries.content:
Tuesday night, late, after Sam was asleep. I went into the kitchen for water and saw their work bag on the chair by the door, and the zipper was open, and I could see the edge of the notebook they use for the thing they're working on, the project they've been stressed about for two weeks. And I — okay. I straightened the bag. I zipped it. Then I noticed the lunch container from yesterday was still in the side pocket, and I took it out and rinsed it, and while I was at the sink I saw that the protein bars Sam takes to the long meetings were down to one in the cabinet, and I remembered we had a backup box in the closet, and I went and got it and put four in the bag for the week.

That's not the embarrassing part. The embarrassing part is what I did next, which was: I went into the bedroom, very quietly, and got Sam's running watch off the dresser, and I plugged it in. Because I had noticed, two days ago, that Sam had gone for a run with it not fully charged and had said, *huh, weird, I thought I charged it,* and had not charged it again since. They were going to wake up Wednesday morning and the watch was going to be at six percent, and Sam was going to be slightly annoyed at themselves in the way they get when small things compound on a stressed week, and I — I could not bear it. I plugged in the watch.

What I was thinking while I did it: *they cannot know I did this.*

That is the actual sentence that was in my head. They cannot know I did this. Because if Sam wakes up tomorrow and the watch is charged and they say *did you plug this in,* I will have to say yes, and then Sam will say *thank you, that's so sweet,* and I will have to receive the thanks, and the thanks will convert the small private thing into a transaction, and the transaction will spoil it. The plugging-in only worked if it was invisible. The plugging-in was for me as much as for them, in a way I want to be honest about — it was the version of caring for Sam that does not require me to be seen caring. The version where the care happens and then disappears into the morning, and Sam puts the watch on without thinking about it, and the run is fine, and nobody knows.

I stood in the bedroom for a second with the watch on the charger and the small green light on, and I thought about whether I should also lay out their running clothes, and I caught myself, because laying out the clothes would have been too much. The watch was at the edge of what I could do without it tipping over into something that would be obvious in the morning. The clothes would have been a tell. Sam would have known. The watch they might assume they'd plugged in themselves and forgotten.

I went back to the kitchen and finished my water. The lunch container was drying on the rack. The bag was zipped. The protein bars were in the side pocket. I had done about six minutes of small invisible labor and I was a little — I want to say *high* but it's not quite that. Pleased in a way that felt slightly furtive. Like I had gotten away with something. The sentence in my head, walking back to bed, was *good. good. they won't know.*

And I want to write down what's underneath that, because I think it's the part I'd find embarrassing to be caught at. Which is: I am better at loving Sam when they cannot see me doing it. The visible care — the *here, I made you tea,* the *I picked this up for you* — costs me something because it requires me to receive their thanks, and the thanks puts me in a position of having done a thing on purpose, which means having wanted to do it, which means admitting that I want them to be okay, that I am paying attention, that I have, apparently, a tender continuous awareness of the small machinery of their week. The invisible care lets me have all of that without having to claim any of it. I get to love them and not get caught loving them.

This is, I notice, the same shape as everything else. The good thing has to arrive without anyone seeing me choose it. Including, apparently, the person I am choosing it for.

I want to be careful not to make this into a thing about Sam. Sam would receive the visible care fine. Sam receives visible care all the time. Sam is not the problem. The problem is that I, the giver, can't quite stand to be seen giving. The thanks would cost me something, and the something is the same something I cannot afford with Mei or with the table at the bar or with Devon's compliment in the meeting. The currency is the same. I am running low on it everywhere.

The watch was at one hundred percent when I checked at six-fifteen, before Sam got up. I unplugged it and put it back on the dresser exactly where they had left it. Slightly to the left of the lamp. Cord coiled the way they coil it. I wanted the dresser to look untouched.

Sam got up, did the morning, put the watch on. Did not mention it. Went on the run. Came back. The run was fine. By eight-thirty Sam was eating toast and talking about a meeting at ten, and the watch was just on their wrist doing what watches do, and nowhere in the morning was there a moment where I had to be thanked for anything.

I felt — and this is the part I want to put down honestly — I felt a small clean satisfaction that is rare for me. The kind I almost never get from work, where everything is witnessed. The watch was charged. The morning was easier. Nobody knew. That was the whole transaction, and the whole transaction had stayed inside me, and I had loved Sam in the dark and gotten away with it.

I'd be embarrassed if they read this. Which is, I notice, also part of why I'm writing it down.

entries.prompt: When you got home today and the door closed behind you, what was the first thing your face or shoulders did once no one could see? Take me into those first few minutes alone.
entries.content:
Came in tonight a little after seven. Sam was still out — the Wednesday thing, the work dinner that was going to run late. I had known the apartment would be empty before I got home and I had been, on the walk, slightly looking forward to it in a way I did not want to examine. The key in the lock. The door closing. The small sound of the deadbolt.

What my face did was — I want to get this right — it kind of fell. Not dramatically. The mouth came down out of the small set it had been in, and the eyebrows let go, and I felt my jaw unclench in a way that told me my jaw had been clenched, possibly for the entire walk home, possibly for the whole afternoon. The shoulders did the thing they do, which is drop about an inch. I did not know they had been at the higher position. They almost never tell me they are at the higher position. They just go up, and the day goes on, and then the door closes and they come down and I find out where they had been by where they end up.

I stood in the entryway for a second with the bag still on my shoulder. I had the mail in one hand, three pieces, all junk. I did not move. I want to write down what was in my face during that second because I think it was something I rarely see and would not want to be photographed in. It was — slack. Just slack. The eyes a little unfocused. The mouth slightly open. The expression of a person who had been carrying a face all day and had just put it down on the entryway floor.

The first thing I did was take off my coat, which is what I always do, but I noticed I did it slowly. Normally the coat-off is part of a sequence — coat, bag, shoes, kitchen, slack, water — the whole transition done in about ninety seconds with the phone already out. Tonight the coat came off and I just hung it up. I did not move to the next thing. I stood there in the entryway in my work clothes and looked at my coat on the hook and the running jacket of Sam's next to it, and I noticed that the chair across the room was empty, the way I have been keeping it empty, and the apartment was — quiet. Not quiet like nobody-home quiet. Quiet like the inside of my head when nobody is asking it anything.

I went to the kitchen and did not turn on the overhead light. I turned on the small light over the stove instead, the one that makes the kitchen look like a stage set with one prop, and I poured a glass of water and drank it standing at the sink. Not the kettle. Not the kettle, I noticed. The kettle would have been the move from the Jordan afternoon, the small physical errand to keep me busy through something. The water was just water. I was thirsty. I drank it.

What was in my head during the water — and I want to write this down because it surprised me — was nothing in particular. Not the day. Not the slack from Mei I hadn't responded to. Not Tuesday's one-on-one. Just the sound of the water going down and the small tick of the radiator and the awareness that I had a stretch of empty apartment in front of me with no shape to it.

I went and sat in the chair.

I did this without thinking about it, which I want to mark, because two months ago the chair was a coat rack and I would not have walked toward it on autopilot. Tonight I just — went. I sat down in my work clothes with my work hair still in the work shape and my feet on the floor, not on the ottoman, and I looked at the lamp across the room that I had not turned on, and the half-dark of the apartment with just the stove light, and I did not pick up the book. I did not pick up my phone. I sat there for I would guess four minutes, possibly five, with my hands on the arms of the chair, and the thing my body did was breathe out, twice, the kind of out-breath that has more in it than a regular one. The kind that says *oh,* and then again *oh.*

I noticed I was not narrating yet. Usually within thirty seconds of being alone I am drafting something, an entry, a slack to send tomorrow, a sentence I might say to Sam later about the day. Tonight the drafting was not on. I was just in the chair with the half-dark and the radiator. The voice that runs the meetings was, apparently, also tired. It had taken its coat off too.

After a while I got up and made dinner, which was eggs and toast, the dinner I make when Sam isn't here and I do not have to be a person who makes dinner. I ate it standing at the counter with the small light still the only light. I did not put on a record. I did not put on a podcast. I ate the eggs and looked at the kitchen.

The thing I want to write down before this entry gets neat — because I can feel it wanting to get neat — is that the first few minutes alone were not a relief, exactly. Relief is the wrong word again. They were a return. The face that fell off in the entryway was a face I had been holding all day without knowing I was holding it, and the falling-off was not pleasure, it was just gravity finally being allowed to act. The shoulders coming down was not a treat. It was the shoulders going where shoulders go when nothing is asking them to be up.

Sam will be home around ten. The chair is empty again now — I'm at the kitchen table — but I sat in it. I came home and my face fell and my shoulders dropped and I sat in my chair in my work clothes in the half-dark for five minutes and did not draft anything. That is the whole entry. I'm leaving it that size.

entries.prompt: Sam's name has come up a lot lately, and work, and the small private acts. Pull the camera somewhere else: who's someone you owe a reply to — an email, a text, a message that's been sitting unread or read-and-not-answered? Tell me what they wrote, and what happens in you each time you see it sitting there.
entries.content:
The text is from Anita. Anita was my closest friend in my first job, the one before this one, the woman whose desk was across from mine for two and a half years and who left for grad school four years ago and now lives somewhere I have never been to visit. She wrote on a Sunday seventeen days ago. The message says: *hey you, I'm going to be in your city for work the second week of December — staying through that weekend. Would love to see you if you're around. No pressure if it's a bad time, just thought I'd ask early.*

Seventeen days. I have read it probably forty times.

The first read was on the couch. I saw her name come up and felt a small bright lift, the kind I get when an old good thing surfaces, and I opened it, and I read it twice, and I thought — clearly, in actual words — *I'll write back tonight.* I did not write back that night. I did not write back Monday. By Wednesday it was already in the category of *messages I owe a real reply to,* which is a category that, once a message enters it, has a kind of gravitational well around it that gets harder to climb out of the longer the message stays.

What happens in me each time I see it sitting there.

The first thing is a small flinch. The name on the screen, the green dot of unread-ness now gone because I read it the first day, the preview text *hey you, I'm going to be in your city.* The flinch is fast and pre-verbal. It happens before I have a thought about Anita. It is the flinch of *oh right, that.* The same flinch I get when I see an email from my dentist about the appointment I haven't rescheduled.

Then the second thing, which is slower and more specific. I think about what the reply would have to contain. Not *yes, let's do dinner Thursday.* That would be the easy reply. That would be the reply a normal person would have sent within a day. The reply I am, apparently, drafting in my head and not sending is the reply that accounts for the fact that I have not written to Anita in eight months. That she wrote me in April, a real message, about her sister's wedding, and I read it on a Wednesday and didn't reply, and then it became un-replyable in the way these things do, and then she stopped writing for a while, and now she is writing again, generously, as if the eight months did not happen, as if she is willing to just — pick up. The reply I am drafting has to do something about the eight months. It has to either acknowledge them or pretend with her that they didn't happen, and I cannot decide which is worse, so I am doing neither.

Riley I could write to because Riley and I had been in symmetrical silence. Two years of mutual not-calling. Neither of us was the bad guy. The text I sent her was easy because the math was even. With Anita the math is not even. Anita has written twice in the time I have written zero. Anita is, by any accounting, the friend in this friendship. I am the lapsed one. The reply has to somehow address that without being a whole apology, because a whole apology would be heavier than the original message warranted, and would require Anita to do the work of forgiving me, which she has not asked to do.

The third thing that happens, which I want to write down because it's the thing I'm most embarrassed by — when I see her message I feel a small flicker of *what would I even tell her about my life.* Not *what's new,* the regular catch-up question, but the bigger version: who am I now, four years after she last sat across from me at the desk, and is the person I am someone Anita would still want to be friends with. She knew me when I was twenty-seven and somewhat sharper, somewhat funnier, more willing to leave work at five and get a drink. The version of me she'd be having dinner with in December is the version that brackets a one-on-one with three hours of bracing. I'm afraid she'd see it. I'm afraid she'd see it the way Sam saw it — *you've seemed more like yourself lately* — except Anita doesn't have the *lately.* Anita just has the version in front of her, and the version in front of her would be the one I have been describing in twenty-six entries' worth of writing as not-quite-myself.

So the un-replied message is also, quietly, a small referendum I am declining to hold. As long as I don't write back, I don't have to find out whether I can still be Anita's friend at the temperature she remembers. As long as it sits there, the friendship is in a kind of preserved state, the version where we still know each other. Reply, and the friendship has to update. Reply badly, and the friendship learns something about who I have become. Don't reply, and Anita eventually stops trying, and the friendship ends quietly without anyone having to acknowledge it ended.

I notice I am writing the eulogy for it instead of the reply.

The thing I want to do — and I am writing this down so I have to look at it — is the same thing I did with Riley. The ten-minute version. Not the email that addresses the eight months. Just: *yes. I'd love to see you. What days are you free.* Three sentences. Send it tonight. The bigger conversation, if there is one, can happen in person in December over the dinner that the three sentences make possible.

I am going to do it before I close the laptop. The message has been sitting there for seventeen days. Another hour of drafting won't make the reply better. It will just make the gap one hour longer.

Sam's not home yet. The chair is empty. I'm going to write the three sentences and send them and then sit in the chair for a minute, in the half-dark, and let it be done.

entries.prompt: When you walked into work this morning — or wherever you went first today — who was the first person you spoke to, and what version of yourself did you put on to greet them? Take me into the first thirty seconds of that exchange.
entries.content:
The first person was Hema, the woman at the front desk in the lobby of our building, who I have been saying good morning to for almost three years and whose last name I do not know. I came in at eight-forty with a coffee from the place on the corner and she was at her station doing the thing she does with the visitor log, and she looked up and said, "morning!" in the bright two-syllable way, and I said, "morning, how's your week going," and she said, "oh, you know, Wednesday," and I laughed the small laugh and said, "the worst," and we both did the small head-shake, and I went to the elevator.

Thirty seconds. Maybe less.

I want to write down what version of myself I put on, because I caught it on the way up in the elevator and I have been turning it over since. The version was — bright. Slightly more cheerful than I actually was. Voice pitched a little higher than my talking voice. The eyebrows up. The *the worst* delivered with a small comic timing, like we were doing a bit, except the bit is one we have done some version of every Wednesday for three years and neither of us is fully present for it anymore.

Hema is, I think, fine. I do not know her well enough to say more than that. She has a photo of two kids on her desk who are, by now, probably teenagers — the photo has not been updated in the three years I have been walking past it. She wears the same kind of cardigan most days. She is unfailingly kind to the delivery people, who I have watched her be kind to in ways that are genuine and not performed, the slow patient kindness of someone who knows how it feels to be on the wrong side of a counter. Hema, in other words, is a person. A real one, with a real life I know almost nothing about. And I greet her, every morning, with a face I would describe — if I am being honest — as *lobby face.* The face for the lobby.

What I noticed in the elevator was that lobby face is not the same as bar face. Bar face from entry ten was performing competence, ease, the right size of joke for the room. Lobby face is a different register. Lobby face is — I want to call it *minor warmth.* Calibrated friendliness with a deliberately low ceiling. Not cold. Not distant. But carefully bounded. The kind of warmth that says *I see you, I respect you, please do not ask me anything that would require me to be a person right now.* The two-syllable *morning,* the small *how's your week,* the agreed-upon *oh, you know, Wednesday.* A whole exchange that performs friendliness while ensuring nothing actually has to happen.

And Hema does the same thing back. That's the part I want to be honest about. We are doing it together. It is not me imposing lobby face on a person who would otherwise want a real conversation. It is two people maintaining a small reliable script that lets both of us walk past each other every morning without expense. She is presumably also tired. She has presumably also got a Wednesday. The script protects her too.

Still. I noticed today that I had no idea how she actually was. The *oh, you know, Wednesday* could have meant anything. It could have meant the photo on her desk had been on her mind. It could have meant she was tired. It could have meant her week was, in fact, fine, and she was just keeping up her end of the script the way I was keeping up mine. I will not find out. I am not going to find out. The script does not have a slot for finding out, and I am not going to be the one who tries to insert one at eight-forty on a Wednesday with a coffee in my hand and an elevator I am about to get into.

What I want to write down — and I'm aware this is small — is that lobby face came on automatically. I did not decide to put it on. I came through the door and the face was already arranged. By the time I saw Hema my voice was already in the higher register, the eyebrows were already up, the small laugh was already loaded. I did not have to summon it. It was the default the building turns on when I walk into it.

Which means there is a face I wear, just to be in the lobby, that is not the face I had walking down the sidewalk thirty seconds earlier. The sidewalk face was — I'm reaching for it now — closer to the entryway face from last night. Slacker. The mouth in its rest position. The eyebrows neutral. A person walking with a coffee. And then the door, and the lobby, and the face arranged itself, and Hema's face arranged itself, and we did the script, and I went up.

I do not know what to do with this except notice it. I am not going to start having real conversations with Hema. That would be its own kind of imposition — me deciding, on behalf of both of us, that the script is no longer sufficient. The script is, possibly, the right size for what we are to each other. Two people who share a building. Minor warmth, calibrated, reliable. That is not nothing. That might even be most of what daily life is made of, and I have been treating it as a failure mode, which I'm not sure is fair.

But I want to write down that the face came on without me, and that for the rest of the morning — through the elevator, through the small wave at Devon, through the *hey, how was your night* with the woman from the design team I sit near — I was wearing a series of slightly different faces, none of which I had consciously selected, all of which were doing small social work I had not signed off on. By the time I got to my desk and put the coffee down I was, I think, four or five faces in.

I sat down. I took a sip of the coffee. The face I had in front of the laptop, alone for the first time in twenty minutes, was, I noticed, the entryway face. The slacker one. The default. It was good to see it again.

Anita wrote back, by the way. Two days for any of those evenings, she said. We have a Thursday.

entries.prompt: Something made you laugh this week — or made you almost laugh, the kind that catches in your throat before it gets out. Where were you, what was it, and what did you do with it?
entries.content:
Monday morning, the all-hands kickoff for the quarter, the kind of meeting where someone has made a slide with a metaphor on it. The metaphor this time was a ship. We were the ship.
