There was a small clip-art ship on the slide, sailing toward a small clip-art horizon, and the horizon had the word *VISION* on it in a font that was trying hard. Mei was presenting. She did not make the slide. I know she did not make the slide because I have seen Mei's slides, and they are spare, and this one had what I can only describe as a sun.

She got to the slide and there was a small pause. Maybe a half-second. And then she said, in the voice she uses when she is being professional about something she would rather not be professional about, "and as you can see, we are the ship."

That was it. That was the line. *We are the ship.*

I made a sound. I want to be clear about what kind of sound. It was not a laugh. It was the noise that happens when a laugh tries to come out of a closed mouth and gets routed through the nose at the last second — a small sharp exhale, the kind that makes the shoulders move once. The camera was on. I watched my own square in the grid jerk, very slightly, and then settle. My face stayed mostly composed. The sound was the only tell.

And then — this is the part I want to write down — Devon's square, two columns over, did the same thing. Half a second after mine. The same small jerk. He had his hand near his face like he was thinking, but I could see the corner of his mouth doing something it should not have been doing. He was watching me. Or he had heard the sound. Or he had, independently, been about to do it himself and my doing it had set him off. I don't know which. What I know is that for about a second and a half there was a small invisible wire between my square and his square in the grid, and we were both holding the laugh at our respective ends of it, and Mei was continuing to talk about the ship.

I did not look at his square again. Looking would have killed me. I looked at Mei's slide instead and tried to make my face into the face of a person interested in a ship. I half-succeeded. The laugh, by then, had gone from a thing trying to get out to a thing sitting hot in my chest, the way the *how dare you* sat hot in my chest with my mom, except this one was the opposite flavor. This one was the kind of hot that wants to be shared. That is most of what makes it hard to swallow — not the funny, but the wanting-to-share. A laugh by yourself you can put away. A laugh that knows another person also has it is a different animal.

The meeting went on. Mei did the rest of the slides. I did not look at Devon. I think I was actually quieter than usual in the chat for the rest of the hour, because any move I made toward saying anything risked the laugh leaking back in.

What I did with it, after — this is the part I want to put down honestly. I slacked Devon. Forty minutes after the meeting ended, when the heat had gone down and I could be normal about it. I sent him *we are the ship,* nothing else, and then I closed the slack and got a glass of water because I could feel myself wanting to draft a longer message, a whole bit, a whole *did you see her face when she said it* riff, and I did not want to do the riff over slack. The riff over slack would die. *We are the ship* by itself, four words, was the cereal aisle version. The riff would be the bar version, the produced one.

He wrote back maybe a minute later. *please don't.* That was the whole reply. *please don't.* And I did the nose-laugh again, alone at my desk this time, and it came out a little louder because there was no grid to perform for.

That was the moment. That was the laugh. *Please don't.*

I want to note something that surprised me, because I keep finding things that surprise me lately and I want to keep marking them. The laugh in the meeting was not, in any way, mean. I like Mei. I know Mei did not make the slide. I know Mei was, in real time, doing the small dignified work of selling a ship metaphor she did not write, and the half-second pause before *we are the ship* was her doing the work, and the small pause was what made the line funny — she had read the slide, she had felt about it what we felt about it, and she had then said the line anyway, because that was her job. The laugh was at the situation, not at her. If anything it was, very faintly, *with* her. The half-second was hers. We were laughing in the same direction.

Which means, I notice — and I am trying to say this without making it a thesis — that for ninety seconds during that meeting I was in a room with Mei without the second conversation underneath. The *please don't* with Devon afterward extended it by another minute. I was not, in that stretch, bracing. I was just in a meeting where something was a little funny and a person I work with had also caught it. The Friday tax was, briefly, not being charged.

I noticed it later, on the walk to get a sandwich. *Oh. That was — easy.* The kind of easy I do not normally get to have at work. The kind that the cereal aisle has, and the dry cleaner had, and that I had been treating as a thing only available outside the building.

I am not going to make this a project. I can feel the doc forming. *Find more ships.* No. The ship found me. I just want to write down that it did, and that Devon was on the other end of the wire, and that *please don't* is, for the next little while, going to be the funniest two words I have read all week.

entries.prompt: When you got dressed this morning, was there a small decision in front of the closet — this shirt or that one, these shoes, the jacket or no jacket — where you felt yourself choosing for an audience? Who was the audience in your head, and what did you end up wearing?
entries.content:
The audience this morning was Mei, which is so embarrassing to write down that I almost want to skip the entry and pick a different one. But Mei was the audience. I can be honest about it here.

I was in front of the closet at seven-fifty, coffee in hand, and I had two shirts out on the bed — the gray one with the small collar, which is the shirt I think of as *the shirt,* the one that I know works on me, that fits the way clothes fit when the dressing-room math is working in your favor. And the rust-colored sweater I bought last week. Not the one from the website. A different one, found in person at the place near the bookstore, similar color, less expensive, no story attached to it. I had worn it once on a Saturday, with Sam, around the apartment, and I had not yet worn it to work.

The Tuesday one-on-one with Mei was at eleven. That was the fact organizing the morning. It had been organizing the morning since I woke up at six-fifteen, even though I had been pretending it wasn't. I had checked the calendar twice. I had drafted, in my head, the answer to *how's the week going.* I had been bracing in the small low way I had promised myself, in entry sixteen, I would notice when I was doing it. I was noticing. I was also still doing it. The noticing did not, today, stop the bracing — it just made the bracing slightly more lit up.

So in front of the closet, with the two shirts on the bed, the question I was actually asking was: *what does Mei need to see today.* Not what do I want to wear. What does Mei need to see, on a Tuesday in a one-on-one I have been bracing for since six-fifteen, to have her opinion of me remain warm.

The gray shirt was the safe answer. The gray shirt is what the version of me Mei first met would wear. Sharp, slightly formal, no risk in it. The gray shirt has a small built-in alibi — it announces that I am taking the meeting seriously without announcing anything else about me. It is a costume the second conversation prefers, because the second conversation likes to have as little extra information about me in the room as possible. The fewer signals, the fewer places for Mei to revise her opinion downward. Gray shirt, neutral pants, the thing.

The sweater was the harder answer. The sweater is — I want to be honest — the sweater is a color. It is rust and brown and slightly warm and it is, on me, a thing people would notice. Not in a *whoa* way. In a *that color is good* way. The sweater would have been a small visible choice. Wearing it would have meant walking into the one-on-one as a person who had made a choice that morning, who had picked a color, who was, in some small way, present in her own clothes.

I picked the gray shirt. I want to write that down before I tell the rest of the entry, because I do not want to construct a story where I bravely chose the sweater. I picked the gray shirt at maybe seven-fifty-three. I put it on. I looked in the mirror. The shirt looked the way it always looks. Fine. Correct. The shirt of a person who is taking the meeting seriously.

And then I stood there for a second and I thought — and this is the part I want to mark — *who is the audience.*

It was Mei. I knew it was Mei. The audience was not the team, not Devon, not myself. The audience was one specific woman who was going to be on a video call with me at eleven and who, if I am being honest with myself in the kitchen, would not actually notice what I was wearing. Mei does not notice clothes. I have been working with her for two years and I could not, if you asked me, tell you what she wore yesterday. The audience in my head was not Mei-as-she-actually-is. The audience was Mei-as-I-imagine-her, the high-stakes version of her that lives behind my sternum, the one whose opinion I am forever pre-running.

I took the gray shirt off.

I want to be careful here because the move I made next is small and I do not want to inflate it. I put the sweater on. Not as a triumph. Not as a *take that, second conversation.* Just because I had caught the audience in my head and I did not, today, want to dress for them. I wanted to dress for the version of me who likes the color rust. The four a.m. picture, in entry twenty-two. The sweater on the chair. I put it on and looked in the mirror and it looked — fine. It looked like me. A me who had, apparently, chosen a color this morning.

The one-on-one was at eleven. Mei did not mention the sweater. Mei did not, as far as I could tell, register the sweater. Mei asked how the week was going and I gave a real answer, not the brightest version, just the regular version, and we talked about the doc and the cross-team thing for twenty minutes and the meeting ended on time. Nothing happened. The sweater was just a sweater. The audience in my head got no material to work with, because the audience in my head had been the one running the show, and I had, for once, put her in the back row.

Devon slacked at noon. *new sweater?* he said. With the eyes emoji. I sent back *yes,* one word, and closed the laptop and went to get lunch.

On the walk I noticed I was warm. The sweater is warmer than the gray shirt. That was, also, a reason to wear it. I had not let myself include that in the morning math. The sweater is warm. It is November. I picked the warmer thing and it kept me warm. That is the whole entry. I am leaving it that size.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you felt genuinely lonely — not alone, lonely — even if you were around people? Take me into where you were and what was happening just before it landed.
entries.content:
Saturday night, the dinner at Theo and Jess's. Eight people at the table, the good kind of dinner — long, candles burning down, Theo had made the lamb thing he makes, someone had brought a wine that everyone made small approving sounds about. Sam was next to me. Devon and his partner were across. Theo and Jess at the heads, two of their friends I had met before but did not know well filling in the rest. The conversation was the conversation you want a Saturday night dinner to be. Loud in places, quiet in others, the kind where someone says something that lands and the table does the small shared laugh and somebody refills the glass next to them without being asked.

It landed somewhere around the third course. I had been on — fine, I had been on, the way I am on, telling a story about a thing at work that wasn't really a story so much as a structured bit, and the table had laughed at the right places, and I had felt the small good feeling of having delivered. And then the conversation moved to one of Jess's friends, the woman to my left, talking about something her sister had done, and the table tilted toward her, and I sat back with my wine and was, suddenly, not on.

That was when it landed. In the small gap where I was off-camera, so to speak, and the conversation was happening two seats over, and my face could go to its rest position for the first time in maybe forty minutes.

I want to describe what it felt like because I am still surprised by it. It was not that I felt left out. The table was not excluding me. Sam's hand was on my knee under the tablecloth. Devon had, ten minutes earlier, looked at me across the table and done the small face that meant *we are having the same private opinion about this wine.* I was, by every external measure, included. Loved, even, in the warm low way a good dinner table loves the people sitting at it. The loneliness was not about being unincluded.

It was about — I'm trying to find this — it was the realization, sitting there with the wine, that the version of me that had told the story about work was not the version of me who had been in the chair in the half-dark on Wednesday. Those two people had been at the table tonight and only one of them had spoken. The story-telling one had done all the work. The chair one — the one who had let her face fall in the entryway, the one who was writing in the journal at the kitchen table, the one who had put on the sweater that morning because she had caught the audience in her head — that one had not been allowed at the dinner. That one had stayed home.

And the loneliness was for her. For the one I had left at home. I was at a table with seven people who like me and I was missing a version of myself I had not brought.

I want to write down something specific that happened, because the abstract version is too tidy. Jess's friend, the woman to my left, was talking about her sister, and at some point she said something — I don't even remember the exact phrase — about how her sister was *finally letting herself want things.* That was the phrase. *Finally letting herself want things.* And I felt my chest do a small thing, a small *oh,* the way it had done at four a.m. with the sweater picture, the way it had done when my dad said the volunteer thing. A small *oh* that wanted to come out as a real sentence. Something like *yes, I know what that is, I am also trying to do that, will you tell me more about your sister.*

What came out instead was *mm.* Just *mm.* Knowing nod. Wine to the lips. The conversation kept going. The woman moved on to a different part of the story. The *oh* in my chest had nowhere to go and so it stayed there, and then a minute later somebody asked me a question about the cross-team work and I came back online and answered it, and the rest of the dinner went the way the dinner was going to go.

But the loneliness arrived in that small interval. Between the *finally letting herself want things* and the *mm.* The interval was maybe two seconds. In those two seconds I knew very precisely that there was a sentence I could have said that would have changed the temperature of the seat I was in, and I did not say it, and the not-saying was the loneliness. Not the absence of people. The presence of people on the other side of a wall I was, in real time, electing to keep up.

Sam noticed something. I think Sam noticed something. On the walk home, both of us a little drunk, Sam said, "you got quiet at the end." Not as a question. Just as a thing they had observed. I said, "yeah. I was tired." Which was true and was also the workload sentence, the *more like yourself* deflection, the move I keep making. Sam squeezed my hand and we walked the rest of the way home without talking.

I lay in bed afterward with the ceiling and I thought about the woman's sister, the one I will never meet, who is finally letting herself want things. And I thought about the version of me at the table who could have said *me too,* and didn't. And the loneliness came back, very quietly, a small low hum in the dark.

I do not know what to do with it except write it down. The journal gets the *me too.* The table got the *mm.* That is the gap, again. Smaller than it was in entry one. Still there.

entries.prompt: There's a small thing you do well — a skill, a habit, a way of handling something — that you'd never quite claim out loud. What is it, and when did you last use it?
entries.content:
The thing I do well is endings. I am good at the end of a meeting.

I want to write that down and look at it because I have never said it to anyone and I have, I realize, known it for years.

What I mean is the last ninety seconds of a call. The part where the substantive work is done and the conversation could either dribble out or land. Most meetings dribble. Somebody says *cool, I think that's it,* and somebody else says *yeah, great, thanks all,* and people unmute to say bye and the squares blink off one at a time and everyone leaves the call holding a slightly different version of what was decided. I notice this happens and I have, somewhere along the way, started to fix it. Not as a system. Just as a thing my mouth does in the last ninety seconds when I can feel the meeting wanting to dribble.

I summarize. I do it lightly. *Okay, so just to make sure I have it — we're going with the second option, Devon's going to draft the thing by Thursday, I'll loop in the design team, and we'll regroup next week.* Three or four sentences. The shape of what we just decided, said back to the room. And then I do the small specific ask — *Mei, does that match what you have?* — and Mei says yes, or adjusts one piece, and the meeting ends with everyone holding the same version. People unmute and say bye and the call ends and the next thing happens.

I last used it Tuesday. The cross-team kickoff, six people, the kind of meeting that should take forty-five minutes and was at fifty-two and showing no signs of stopping. Somebody had just said *so, I think we're aligned,* which is the sentence that means *we are not aligned but I would like to leave.* I waited a beat to see if anyone would catch it. Nobody did. I came in and said the summary. Three sentences. Action items attached to names. Asked the most senior person on the call — not Mei this time, somebody from another org — if it matched what she had. She said *yes, exactly,* with a small relieved tone I have learned to recognize, the tone of someone who was also worried we were going to leave the call un-aligned and is grateful that someone else did the small work of stopping it.

Meeting ended at fifty-four minutes. Everyone had the same version. I closed the laptop and went and made tea.

I have done this, I am realizing as I write, in some form for probably six years. It started in my first job, where the meetings were worse and the consequences of un-aligned meetings landed on me directly because I was the most junior person on the call and the cleanup was my job. I learned to do the summary because doing the summary in the meeting was less work than doing the cleanup after. By the time I got to this job it was automatic. I do it without deciding to. The shape of a meeting that is about to dribble is, to me, as obvious as a kettle starting to whistle, and the summary is the move that takes it off the heat.

Why I would not claim it out loud, I want to be honest about. The first reason is that it sounds like nothing when you say it. *I'm good at ending meetings* is not a sentence you can put in a performance review. *I summarize at the end of calls* is not a skill, technically. It's a verbal tic. It would land, in a self-assessment, the way *I'm a hard worker* lands, which is to say not at all.

The second reason is that the move only works if it is invisible. The summary works because it sounds like I am just checking my own understanding. *Just to make sure I have it.* The slight self-deprecation is the lubricant. If I claimed it as a skill — *I am the person who lands the meeting* — the move would stop working, because the move requires the room to think it is doing the landing collectively while I am, quietly, doing it for them. The invisibility is part of the technology. Like the watch on the charger. The care that doesn't get caught.

The third reason, and this is the one I want to write down, is that claiming it would mean noticing that I am, in some small way, good at my job. Not in the *I am exhausting myself trying to be sharp* way that Mei is the audience for. In a real way. A way that is mine. Six years of a habit I cannot turn off, that improves the quality of every meeting I am in, that other people benefit from without knowing where it came from. That is — I don't have a clean word — that is real competence. The quiet kind. The kind that does not require anyone to grade it because it works whether or not it is graded.

If I claimed it I would have to admit that there is a category of *things I do well* that has nothing to do with whether Mei is recalibrating. The category would exist independently of her opinion. And the second conversation, the one that runs underneath the first, requires that no such category exist — that everything I do at work be up for re-evaluation at any moment. The summary skill, if I let myself name it, punches a small permanent hole in that. *This one, at least, you can't take from me. I have been doing it since before I met you. I will be doing it after.*

I am writing this down, for now, in the journal, where the truer things go. Not in a self-assessment. Not out loud to Mei. Just here.

The Tuesday meeting ended at fifty-four minutes. The senior woman from the other org slacked me an hour later. *thanks for pulling that together at the end, I had to run.* I read it twice and then I did the thing I do, which was write back *no problem!* with the small wave. The exclamation point, the wave, the deflection. I did not say *yeah, I do that. That's a thing I do.*

But I noticed, this time, that I had done it. The summary in the meeting, and the deflection in the slack. Both moves visible to me in the same hour. That is more than I had a month ago. I will take it.

entries.prompt: Pick a piece of advice you've given someone recently — at work, to a friend, in passing. What did you tell them, and would you actually take it yourself?
entries.content:
Devon, last Thursday, in the small kitchen by the printers where the coffee is bad but free. He had been quiet in our standup and I caught him refilling his mug and asked if he was okay, and he said — in the slightly dry way he says serious things — that he'd been spinning on a doc for a week, couldn't get it right, kept rewriting the opening, was starting to think the whole frame was wrong. He had the look of a person who had spent the morning staring at a cursor.

What I told him, in maybe forty seconds at the coffee machine: send it. Send the version you have. The doc is fine. Sometimes the only way to find out what's actually wrong with a draft is to put it in front of someone who hasn't been staring at it for six days, and the rewriting at this point is just a way to avoid finding out. Just send it. Mei will have notes. The notes are not the disaster. The disaster is another week of you not sleeping over a doc.

I delivered this warmly. I think I touched his arm at one point, which I don't usually do. Devon laughed the small grateful laugh and said *yeah, you're right, you're right,* and went back to his desk and — I watched him do it from across the floor — sent the doc within about ten minutes. Mei wrote back the next morning with three small notes and one larger reframe. He did the reframe. The doc went out Friday. He slacked me *thank god you said that, I was going to keep going.*

I felt, when I read the slack, the small clean satisfaction of having been useful. Real satisfaction. The kind I don't get from being told my own work is good. I had said the right thing at the right time and a person I like had been spared three more days of the bad thing, and I knew the advice was correct because I had watched it work.

Now. Would I take it.

Reader, I would not.

I have, on my own laptop right now, two drafts I have been sitting on for longer than Devon's doc was sitting. One is the cross-team proposal Mei asked me to put together, which I have rewritten the opening of — I just counted — five times. The other is a smaller thing, a note to my skip-level about a process change I think we should make, that I have been *almost ready to send* for, conservatively, three weeks. Both of them are fine. I know they are fine. I know, with the same certainty I had about Devon's doc, that the rewriting is no longer improving them, that I am polishing them as a way of not finding out what someone else thinks.

I have not sent either of them. I will, probably, not send the skip-level one tonight, even though I am writing this entry specifically about how I should.

I want to be honest about why the advice is easy to give and impossible to take. When Devon was stuck, what I could see clearly was that the cost of not sending was higher than the cost of imperfect notes. Another week of spinning was worse than three small comments from Mei. The math was obvious from the outside. I could do the math for him in forty seconds because his doc was not load-bearing for my sense of whether I am a person.

My drafts are load-bearing. That's the thing. The cross-team proposal is not just a doc. It's the thing Mei has been routing my way since the one-on-one where I gave her the sayable answer about cross-team scope. It is, in some way I have not let myself look at directly, a test of whether I can hold the larger thing I told her I wanted. If I send it and the notes are big — if Mei comes back with a *let me reframe this with you,* the way she did with the framing in entry one — then the whole arc of *you are growing into bigger work* takes a small hit, and I will have to spend three days reconstructing the version of me that asked for it.

So I rewrite. The rewriting is not really rewriting. It's a way of keeping the doc in the unfindable category. As long as it is not sent, it cannot be revised downward. The doc preserved in draft is, paradoxically, more competent than the doc sent and improved.

This is exactly the move I told Devon not to make. Word for word. *The rewriting is just a way of avoiding finding out.* I said that to him at the coffee machine. I knew it when I said it. I know it now, looking at the proposal in the tab next to this one.

The skip-level note is a smaller version of the same. There the stakes are lower — it's not even about whether the idea is good, it's just about whether I am the kind of person who sends notes to skip-levels with opinions in them. Three weeks of *almost ready* is three weeks of me deciding I am not, today, that person. The doc waits. The person I am asking to become also waits.

I notice the shape from entry thirty-two. The summary at the end of meetings — the thing I am good at, that I won't claim. Versus this — the thing I tell Devon to do, that I won't do. Two versions of the same evasion. The competence I have, I won't name. The advice I give, I won't take. Both moves keep me at a remove from being a person who is, plainly, someone who does the things she does. There is something I am protecting by refusing to inhabit my own positions.

I do not, tonight, know how to fix this. I am not going to make it a doc.

What I am going to do, before I close the laptop, is send the skip-level note. Not the big one. The small one. The ten-minute version of taking my own advice. The cross-team proposal can wait until tomorrow, or the day after, but the skip-level note has been sitting for three weeks and the only thing wrong with it is that I have not pressed send.

I'm going to do it now, before I edit this paragraph, before the part of me that runs the meetings has time to get the door closed.

Sent. Okay. The note is gone. I feel — slightly nauseous, slightly lighter, mostly tired. We will see what comes back.

Devon would be proud. I am not telling him.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you felt jealous — even a flicker of it — about something someone else had or got or pulled off? Where were you when it showed up, and what did you do with it in the moment?
entries.content:
Friday evening, scrolling on the couch before dinner. I was not looking for anything. I had the show on with Sam in the kitchen and I was doing the thing where the phone is in my hand and the thumb is moving and my eyes are on the screen and none of it is really happening. And then a post from someone I went to college with — not a close friend, the kind of person whose name I would recognize at a reunion and not before — with a picture of a book. Her book. The cover, the early copy in her hand, the small bashful caption. *Two years of work. Out in the spring.*

The flicker was instant. Hot at the back of the neck, the same hot wash as Jordan, smaller, sharper. I locked the phone face-down on my thigh.

I want to write down what came up in the next ten seconds because I caught it cleanly this time, the way I have been trying to.

First: *who.* I had to actually go back to figure out who she was. We had taken one class together junior year. She had been quiet in seminar, smart in a low-key way, the kind of person who said something good in week eleven and then disappeared into the last papers. I have not thought about her in eight years. I am not in any meaningful sense in competition with her. She is not Jordan. She is not even on the list.

Second, and this is the part I want to be honest about: the flicker did not care that I am not in competition with her. The flicker did not check the roster. The flicker arrived because someone roughly my age, from roughly my world, had made a thing — an actual thing, with a cover, with her name on it — and I had not. The flicker was not really about her. The flicker was about the shape of the thing. *She did the thing. You did not do the thing. There is a thing, and people do it, and you are not one of those people.*

Third: a small fast inventory of what *the thing* even is, in my head. Because I have not, in any sustained way, wanted to write a book. I do not have a manuscript in a drawer. I do not, when I am being honest, even read enough fiction to have a strong sense of what kind of book I would want to make. The wanting that the flicker pointed at was not for her specific outcome. It was for the *fact of having made something visible.* Two years of work, she said, and then a cover. A receipt. Evidence that the time had been spent and had produced an object. I have spent two years on — what? Decks. Docs. The cross-team thing. The proposal I have rewritten five times. None of it has a cover. None of it would, in five years, be a thing I could hold up.

What I did with it in the moment: I did not put down the phone. I did not, which would have been the move, get up and go help Sam in the kitchen and let the post drift down the feed. I unlocked the phone and went back to her profile and did the small archeology. How long had she been working on it. Who was the publisher. What was it about. I scrolled three years back. I read the captions. I was, I want to be specific, prospecting for reasons it didn't count. *Small press. Niche topic. She probably had connections.* The voice that runs the meetings, doing its meanest work, scanning a stranger's accomplishment for the asterisk.

I caught myself doing it around the third post back. I want to mark this because the catching is, I think, the only real progress. I did not stop scrolling immediately. I scrolled one more, registered that I was scrolling, and then put the phone down face-up on the couch arm. Not face-down. Face-up, where I could see it not being touched.

What was underneath the prospecting, when I let myself look — and this is the part I will write in the journal and not say at the dinner table — was a small ugly sentence. *I used to be the one who would have done this.* Not a book, specifically. But the kind of thing you only make if you are willing to spend two years on a thing nobody asked for. I used to be that person. In college. In my first year out of college. I made things. Small things, bad things, things that did not have covers, but I made them without permission. Somewhere in the last few years I stopped, and I started doing only the things people asked for, and the things people ask for are docs and decks, and docs and decks do not become objects. They become next quarter.

The flicker was envy and it was also — the word is grief. A small grief. Not for her thing. For mine. For the version of me who would have, by thirty-one, made something that had a cover, if I had kept being that person. The flicker was the version-who-would-have looking at the version-who-did, even though we don't know each other.

I went into the kitchen. I told Sam — not the whole thing, just — *somebody from college wrote a book.* Sam looked up from the cutting board and said, *oh yeah? anyone you knew well?* And I said, *not really. I'm a little jealous.* And Sam said, *of the book or of the having-finished-something?* Which is the thing about Sam. They got it on the first try. I said, *the second one,* and Sam said, *yeah, that tracks,* and went back to chopping, and I stood at the counter and watched them for a minute and then started the rice.

I did not get back on the phone. The post did its work and I let it. I noticed, while we ate, that the flicker had not gone away — it was sitting low under the conversation, not loud — and I did not, this time, treat its presence as a failure. It was just there. A small piece of information about something I have been refusing to know.

I am writing this and I can see the proposal tab. Five rewrites. Two years of work that will not, in the spring, be a cover.

I do not know what the object would be, if I made one. I am writing it down anyway, here, where the truer things go. *I want to make a thing that has a cover.* Not for Mei. Not for the table. Some object, eventually, that is mine.

The flicker was useful. I would not have known the sentence otherwise.

entries.prompt: Think about a small gift or favor someone did for you recently — something they didn't have to do. What was it, and what went through your head when you realized they'd done it?
entries.content:
Wednesday, mid-morning. I had a meeting at ten that I had been mildly dreading — the quarterly review with the partner team, the one where Mei would be there and so would two people from the other org I have only met on calls and where the deck I had prepared was, I knew, just barely enough. I had been pre-running it since I sat down at my desk at eight-forty.

At nine-fifty Devon slacked me. *hey — you have a sec before the ten? sending you something.* Two minutes later, a doc in my inbox. The title was *cross-team review — devon's notes for context.* Three pages. He had, apparently, sat down at some point yesterday or this morning and written out everything he knew about the two people from the other org — what they cared about, where they had pushed back in past meetings, a small note about the senior one's preferred framing, a flag about a project of theirs he thought we should reference. At the bottom he had written, *not sure if useful, you probably know all this, just thought I'd put it somewhere.*

I read it twice. The first read was fast, scanning for whether I needed to absorb anything new before the meeting. I did. Two of the things he flagged I had not known. The second read was slower and was when the thing landed.

He did not have to do this. Devon was not in the meeting. Devon had no stake in the meeting going well. He had spent — I cannot estimate, maybe forty minutes? maybe an hour? — writing out context for a meeting he was not attending so that I would walk into it slightly less unarmed. He had done it quietly. He had framed it as *not sure if useful,* which is the same lubricant I use when I do the meeting summary, the small self-deprecation that lets the gift land without making the receiver have to do too much with it. He had used my own move on me.

What went through my head, in order. First — and I want to be honest — the small reflexive flinch. *Why is he doing this. What does he want. Did Mei put him up to it. Is this a setup where I'm supposed to do something back.* The same diagnostics I ran on Devon's compliment in entry two, the cross-examination of a thing offered as a gift. I scanned the doc for the catch. There was no catch. The doc was just a doc. He had written it because he had thought it would help.

Second, slower: *he was paying attention.* That was the sentence that came up. He had noticed that I had this meeting. He had noticed the two people from the other org. He had noticed — and this is the part that did something to me — that I was probably, on a Wednesday morning, doing what I always do, which is bracing. He had not addressed the bracing. He had just done a small thing that made the bracing slightly less expensive. He had, like Sam with the watch, loved me in the dark. Except he had signed his name to it.

Third: a small hot thing in the throat, the kind that surprised me by being there. Not tears. The thing that comes before tears, the small pressure that says *something just landed and the body is responding before the brain has decided what to do.* I sat there for maybe ten seconds with the doc open and the throat-thing happening, and I noticed I was — I don't know how to put it — I was being cared for at work, in a way that had bypassed the apparatus. The second conversation had not had time to get the door closed. Devon's doc had walked right past it. The care was in before I could screen it.

What I did, of course, was the move. I slacked back *omg this is so generous, thank you, this is so helpful.* Three exclamation points. The brisk warm tone, slightly too bright. Already starting to mail him a small package back. I caught myself with my hands on the keyboard about to type *I owe you,* and I deleted it. I deleted *let me return the favor sometime* too. I made myself send just the first sentence. *omg this is so generous, thank you, this is so helpful.* Hit enter. Closed slack.

I noticed I was a little ashamed of how warm I had felt for the ten seconds before the deflection turned on. As if forty minutes of Devon's time was not enough of an occasion for the throat-thing. As if I was being too much about it. The voice that runs the meetings, fast on the scene: *calm down, it's a context doc, people send context docs.* Except people don't. I have worked with Devon for two and a half years and he has not, before, sent me a context doc for a meeting I had on my own. This was not the regular weather. This was a specific thing he had done on a specific Wednesday for a specific reason, and the specific reason was that he wanted the meeting to go well for me.

The meeting went well. I used two of his notes. The senior woman from the other org responded to the framing he had flagged. I closed the call at ten-fifty-three and sat at my desk for a minute and felt, again, the small warm thing, slightly recovered from the deflection.

I want to write down what I am going to do, because I have learned by now that if I don't write it down I will not do it. Tomorrow morning I am going to slack Devon and say one real sentence. Not *thanks again.* Not *I owe you.* Something like *that doc made the meeting. I don't think you know how much.* And then I'm going to send it without the exclamation point. I'm going to let the sentence be the size it actually is.

The doc is still open in a tab. *not sure if useful, you probably know all this.* The lubricant. He used my own move on me. I am going to remember that for a while.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel impatient with someone — the kind where you were already half-composing your reply while they were still talking? Who were they, what were they saying, and what was the rush underneath it?
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon, on the phone with my mom. The regular call, slightly later than usual because she had been at something at church in the morning. I had it on speaker while I folded laundry on the bed, which is a thing I do that I would not admit to her.

She was telling me about my aunt's kitchen renovation. The contractor situation. The thing with the tile that had come in the wrong color and had to be re-ordered. The fact that my uncle had wanted the island a different way and my aunt had won. There were, I want to be precise, multiple subplots. The tile was its own arc. The island was a separate arc. The contractor had a backstory.

I was already composing my reply by the time she got to the tile.

The reply I was composing was — and this is the part I want to write down honestly — not even a real reply. It was the wrap-up. I was drafting the sentence that would let me get off the phone. *That sounds like such a project, I'm glad it's coming together, listen I should let you go, I have to —* and then I was hunting, while she talked about grout, for the thing I had to. Dinner. A walk. Sam needed something. I was inventing the errand on top of her sentences while she was still saying them.

The folding got faster. I noticed that. My hands were doing the t-shirts in the brisk irritated way, the way that produces slightly worse folds. I caught myself snapping a sleeve into place like the t-shirt had done something.

What she was actually saying was fine. It was fine. My aunt's kitchen is a thing my mom cares about. My aunt is her sister. The tile mattered to my aunt and so it mattered to my mom and she was telling me about it on a Sunday afternoon the way people tell people things on Sunday afternoons. There was nothing wrong with the content. There was nothing wrong with the pace. The pace was my mom's regular pace, which is the pace of a person who is not in a hurry and is talking to her daughter.

The rush was mine. Entirely mine. And I want to look at it because I caught it and I don't fully understand it yet.

The first layer of the rush was the obvious one. I had things I wanted to do with the rest of the afternoon. The chair, the book, a walk before it got dark. The call had started at three-fifteen and we were at three-thirty-five and the afternoon was visibly being used up by tile. *Get to the point* was the sentence underneath. Except — there was no point. The tile was the point. The tile was not a preamble to a more important thing. My mom had not called to tell me a more important thing. My mom had called because Sunday is when we talk and the tile was what was on her mind.

The second layer, which I noticed when I tried to push past the first, was that I was impatient because I did not want to be on the phone with her *as a person.* I wanted to be on the phone with her as a duty. The duty has a shape — fifteen, twenty minutes, the standard catch-up, *love you, talk next week.* The duty I can do while folding laundry. The duty does not require me to be present. What she was doing, by telling me about the tile in real detail, was treating the call as a real call, and a real call requires me to be a daughter on a Sunday afternoon, present, listening, asking the small follow-up questions about my aunt that would let her keep going. I did not want to do that. I wanted to do the duty version and get off.

The third layer, and this is the one I want to write down because it surprised me — under the impatience, very quietly, was the *how you get* call. Five entries ago. Six? The Thursday she said *good, because you know how you get,* and I felt the match strike. I have been, I notice, slightly cooler with her on every call since. Two degrees. Not enough that she would say anything. Enough that I do not let her in past a certain point. The impatience on Sunday was, I think, partly the shape of that coolness. I was rushing her off the phone because letting her stay on the phone meant letting her be a person who could, at any moment, say another *how you get,* and the bracing for the *how you get* is more expensive than the call is worth.

Which means I was rushing her not because the tile was boring but because I was, on some low frequency, defending against a sentence she was not, today, going to say.

She was just telling me about tile.

I caught the rush around the eight-minute mark. I want to be honest that catching it did not, immediately, make the rush stop. The hands kept folding fast. The wrap-up sentence was still half-drafted. What catching it did was let me notice that my mom had, twice in the last minute, paused in a way that was a small invitation. *And the contractor said —* pause. *Your aunt thinks —* pause. The pauses were places where I could have said something. I had been letting them go by, filling them with *mm* and *oh wow,* the lobby-face version, the *mm* that was the same *mm* I gave Jess's friend at the dinner table when she said the thing about her sister.

I sat down on the bed. Stopped folding. Said, "what color did she end up going with."

It was a small question. I did not even particularly care about the answer. But the question was a sentence with shape, and it landed in one of the pauses, and my mom — I could hear her face change, over the phone, eight hundred miles away — said, *oh, it's a really nice green, I'll send you a picture,* in a slightly different voice. The voice of someone who had been talking for a while and had just been told someone was listening.

We talked for another twelve minutes. I did not draft any more wrap-up sentences. The laundry stayed half-folded on the bed. When we got off the phone she said *love you, sweetie,* and I said *love you too,* and I meant it in the regular way, which is — I will say — more than I have meant it on a few of the recent calls.

The chair was still there. The book was still there. The walk happened, just later, in worse light. None of it was harmed by the twelve extra minutes. The rush had been about something else entirely, and the something else is, apparently, still sitting between us, even when she is telling me about tile.

She sent the picture of the green that night. It is, in fact, a really nice green.

entries.prompt: Was there a moment this week when someone asked how you were and you gave the easy answer? Take me into the exchange — who was asking, what you said, and what the longer answer would have been if you'd let yourself give it.
entries.content:
Thursday morning, the elevator. Hema had said her version downstairs and I had said mine, the regular script, and then on the elevator a woman from two floors down — I don't know her name, we have nodded at each other for probably a year — got on at three and we did the small acknowledgement and then she said, "how's your week going?" in the elevator-voice, the one that is asking and not asking.

I said, "good, busy, you know."

She said, "yeah, same, always."

We rode the rest of the way without talking. She got off at five. I went to seven.

That was the exchange. Eight seconds. I want to write it down because I have been, since the Sunday call with my mom, paying slightly more attention to the *mm*s and the brisk replies, and the elevator one was so small and so practiced that I almost missed it. *Good, busy, you know.* Three pieces, none of them load-bearing. *Good* says nothing. *Busy* is the universal alibi, the word that has replaced the other words. *You know* hands the rest back to her, because she does, in fact, know — she has her own version of the same week — and the *you know* tells her she does not need to ask anything else and I do not need to say anything else and we can both just stand in the elevator looking at the numbers.

The longer answer. I have been turning this over since I sat down to write.

The longer answer would have started with the fact that I had not slept well. Sam had been up at three with something — a work thing, an email they could not stop reading — and I had half-woken with them and not gone back under properly until five, and then the alarm at six-forty had felt like it had been delivered to the wrong address. I was on my second coffee already and it was nine-fifteen and the day had the slightly thin quality days have when you have not slept, where everything is a little louder than it should be and your responses arrive a half-second late.

The longer answer would have included that I had sent the skip-level note last week, the small one, and had received a reply on Monday that was warm and substantive and had asked me to come present the idea at the next staff meeting, and I had said yes within ninety seconds, and I have been, since Monday, slightly nauseous about it. The nausea is not bad. It is the nausea of a thing I asked for arriving and now having to be inhabited. The kind I have not let myself feel often, because I have not, often, asked.

The longer answer would have included that Anita comes in two weeks, and I have been thinking about the dinner more than I expected, and that I have started — and I am embarrassed to write this — drafting things to tell her. Not bullet points, exactly. But sentences I want to make sure I say. *I missed you.* *The eight months were on me.* *I want to do this more.* I have not had to draft sentences for a friend in a long time. The drafting is a small private project that lives next to the journal. I do not know yet whether I will say any of them.

The longer answer would have included Devon's doc, which I am still thinking about a week later, and the slack I sent him the next morning — the real one, no exclamation point, *that doc made the meeting, I don't think you know how much* — and that he had written back *I had a feeling,* three words, and that those three words have been sitting in my chest in a small good way since.

The longer answer would have included that I am wearing the rust sweater again, the second time this week, because it is warm and because I caught myself, this morning, almost not wearing it and asked myself why and could not produce a real reason, and put it on.

None of this would have fit in the elevator. That is the obvious thing to say and I want to say it because I do not want to make the elevator into a failure. The woman from two floors down did not ask for the longer answer. *How's your week going* in an elevator at nine-fifteen is not a question, it is a small social gesture, a way of acknowledging that we have shared a year of nods and are now sharing a small box together for forty seconds. The script is correct for the situation. *Good, busy, you know* is the right size.

What I want to write down is that I noticed, in the elevator, that I had a longer answer ready. That is the new thing. A month ago I think the longer answer would not have been there. The longer answer would have been fog, the way it was fog in entry one when Sam asked about the meeting with Mei. Fog, and then the bitter joke wearing the fog as a costume. This morning there was no fog. There were specific sentences I could have said if the room had been different. The skip-level. Anita. Devon's three words. The sweater. I had them. They were inside me, in order, available.

I gave the easy answer because the easy answer was the right answer for an elevator. But I noticed, getting off at seven, that I was a little disappointed that the elevator had been an elevator and not somewhere else. As if the longer answer had been packed and ready and the trip had been cancelled. The longer answer went back in the bag.

I am writing it here because here is, apparently, the room I have for it. The journal is a longer-answer-shaped room. The kitchen with Sam is, sometimes, when I can manage it. Anita's dinner might be. The elevator is not, and that is fine. I am not going to start giving longer answers in elevators. I am just going to know, while I am giving the short one, that the longer one exists, and that this is the thing that has changed.

*Good, busy, you know.* The elevator dinged. I went to my desk. The longer answer rode the rest of the way down with her, unsaid, and is here now, on the page, where it can be the size it is.

entries.prompt: Last weekend you didn't have plans on Saturday — or if you did, pick whichever recent unscheduled stretch comes to mind first. Walk me through how you actually spent it, hour by hour if you can, and where the shape of the day surprised you or didn't.
entries.content:
Saturday. Sam was at the long run again — the half is in three weeks and they're doing the build-up properly this time, which means out the door at seven-thirty, back around eleven-thirty, possibly with bagels, possibly without depending on whether the group goes for them. I knew before I went to bed Friday that Saturday morning was mine. I noticed I went to sleep slightly looking forward to it.

The shape, hour by hour, since the prompt asked.

Seven-thirty to eight-fifteen, in bed after Sam left. Not asleep. Not really awake. The cat next door doing its thing through the wall. I lay there with the light coming in around the curtain and did not pick up the phone, which I want to mark because a month ago I would have. The not-picking-up was not a discipline. It was that the phone was on the dresser and the bed was warm and reaching for it would have required leaving the warm thing to get the colder thing, and I, apparently, did the math.

Eight-fifteen to nine, coffee. The slow way again. Stood at the window. The woman across the street was not watering anything this time. Different woman, actually — I think the watering one is on three and this one was on four, with a kid in pajamas climbing on her while she tried to drink something. I watched them for maybe two minutes. The kid kept putting his hands on her face.

Nine to ten-thirty, the chair. I had the essays still, and I read another one — the one about the bridge had been weeks ago, this was a different one, about a market in a city I have also never been to. The writer had a way of describing the smell of the fish without making it gross, which is its own kind of skill. I read for probably forty minutes and then I just sat. Not reading, not thinking about anything in particular. The light moved across the rug the way it does. The chair was, I noticed, just a chair again. Not a project. Not a small triumph. A green chair I was sitting in on a Saturday morning. The fact that this was unremarkable was, itself, the thing I want to mark. Two months ago sitting in it would have been an event. This morning it was furniture.

Ten-thirty to eleven, restless. I want to write this down because the morning was not all soft light and chair. Around ten-thirty something turned on in me — a small *should be doing something* hum, not loud, but present. I noticed it. I considered making a list. I considered going for a run, even though I do not run. I considered cleaning the bathroom, which did not need cleaning. The hum was looking for somewhere to land. I put the kettle on for more coffee I did not need and stood at the counter while it heated and the hum thinned out without my having to do anything about it. By the time the kettle clicked I had forgotten what I was making. I made the coffee anyway.

Eleven to eleven-forty, walked to the bookstore. Not the used one — the bigger one, on the avenue. I had not planned to go. I just put on shoes and went. I bought nothing. I picked up four books and put all four down. I read the first page of a novel a friend had mentioned months ago and decided against it, which felt, in a small way, like an act. *Decided against.* Not *might come back to.* Just no. The not-buying was not the closed-tab kind from the sweater. It was the kind where I had actually checked and the book wasn't for me. Different category. I noticed the difference on the walk back.

Eleven-forty, home. Sam not back yet. Made eggs. Ate them at the counter standing up. Read four more pages of the essays. The apartment was quiet in the way it gets when nobody has been asking it to be anything for a few hours.

Sam came in at twelve-fifteen with bagels. They had gone for bagels.

Where the shape of the day surprised me, or didn't.

It didn't, mostly. That's the thing I want to write down. The Saturday morning a few weeks ago — the one I wrote about, the four hours of unhurried, the bench with the man and the kid — I had treated as a small event. A stretch I needed to mark because it was rare. This Saturday was, structurally, the same shape. Coffee, chair, walk, eggs. And I did not, this time, narrate it as I went. I did not catch myself composing the entry on the bench, because there was no bench, and there was no entry forming. There was just the morning. I am writing about it now because the prompt asked. If the prompt had not asked I might not have written about it. I might have just had it.

Which is — I am trying to find the right size for this — which is maybe what I have been wanting. Not the morning itself, which was ordinary. The fact that the morning was allowed to be ordinary. That a Saturday with no plans could go by without me having to either fill it with productivity or treat it as an event and bank it as material. It was just a Saturday. The chair was a chair. The walk was a walk. The hum at ten-thirty came and went and did not require a project.

I want to be honest that this is partly the prompt's gift and partly real. The real part is that I sat in the chair without thinking *the chair.* The prompt is making me think *the chair* now. But the sitting was unmarked while it happened. That counts.

Sam asked, eating the bagel, what I'd done. I said, "not much. Read. Walked to the bookstore. Didn't buy anything." Sam said, "good morning?" I said, "yeah. Regular." Sam laughed, a small one, at the *regular,* because they know what regular has cost, and we ate the bagels and I didn't elaborate. I let *regular* be the word.

Anita is in a week and a half. The proposal is still in five rewrites. The skip-level presentation is Tuesday. None of that was in the morning. The morning was just the morning. I am putting it down here, the size it was.

entries.prompt: There's probably something on your to-do list that's been migrating from one day to the next without ever actually getting done. What is it, and what happens in you each time you see it and move it again?
entries.content:
The thing is the dentist.

I have not been to the dentist in — I had to actually count this morning, in the kitchen, holding my coffee — three years and four months. The last appointment was in the summer before the pandemic got weird, with a woman whose name I cannot remember now, who told me my gums were fine but I was grinding my teeth at night and recommended a guard. I did not get the guard. I am, presumably, still grinding.

The task on the list is *call dentist.* It has been on the list, in various forms, for — I want to be honest — at least a year and a half. It has been on paper lists, in the notes app, in the small running thing I keep at the top of my work doc that I am not supposed to keep at the top of my work doc. It has migrated. Mondays it gets re-typed in the new week's column. Sundays sometimes it gets added when I am doing the small Sunday-night list that I pretend is planning and is mostly anxiety. I have moved this task probably forty times. I have not, in those forty times, called the dentist.

What happens in me each time I see it.

The first thing is a small specific kind of dread that is different from the work dread. The work dread has a shape — *Mei is going to,* *the meeting is at,* *the doc needs.* The dentist dread is more diffuse. It is dread plus shame plus a small low *you have let this go and the longer you have let it go the worse the going-back is going to be.* The not-calling has, over a year and a half, generated its own additional content. The original task was *call dentist for a cleaning.* The current task is *call dentist for a cleaning and explain why it has been three and a half years and find out what has gone wrong in your mouth in the interim and probably need three appointments instead of one and possibly the guard situation has gotten worse.* The task has accreted. It is a bigger task now than it was when I first wrote it down, and the bigger it gets the more it justifies not doing it today, because today is not a day for a multi-appointment task, today is a day for the small things.

The second thing is the small fast inventory of why today, specifically, is not the day. *It's after five, the office is closed.* *It's a Saturday, they're not open.* *It's Monday morning, you have the standup, you cannot be on hold.* *It's lunch, they're at lunch.* *It's three-thirty on a Wednesday, by the time you get through the menu the meeting will be starting.* There is, apparently, no window in a week that is the window for calling the dentist. The window does not exist. The window is theoretical. The task is waiting for a window that the calendar does not contain.

The third thing — and I have been trying to look at this directly because I have noticed that the task is getting heavier, not lighter, the longer I do not do it — is that the task has stopped being about the dentist. The dentist is fine. I liked the woman. The office was clean. The cleaning will take forty minutes and I will sit in the chair with the small bib and they will scrape my teeth and I will leave with the small bag of floss. None of that is the problem. The problem is that *call dentist* has become, in my private accounting, a referendum on whether I am the kind of person who takes care of herself. Every time I move it to the next week I am voting, quietly, that I am not. The vote is small but it is daily, and the votes are stacking, and the stack is now visible to me in a way it was not a year ago.

This is the same shape as the chair when it was a coat rack. The chair was a slow accumulation that I did not notice was happening until one Saturday I noticed and the noticing was the start of the un-accumulating. The dentist task is the same. It has been accumulating. I am, today, noticing.

I notice also — and I want to mark this because it is the thing the journal keeps catching me at — that I would not let Devon do this. If Devon told me, at the coffee machine, that he had not been to the dentist in three and a half years and was kind of dreading the call, I would say: *just call. Right now. Pull up the number, call from your desk, leave a voicemail if they don't pick up. The call is ten minutes. The not-calling is a year and a half.* I would say it warmly. I would mean it. Devon would, probably, call.

I am also not going to call right now. I want to be honest about that. I am at the kitchen table on a Sunday evening and the office is closed. The window is, again, theoretical. But what I am going to do — and I am writing this down so it is in the journal and not in my head — is put the number in my phone tonight, before I close the laptop, so that tomorrow morning at nine-fifteen, when I am at my desk with my coffee and the office is open, the only thing between me and the call is my thumb.

The number is — I just looked it up, the office is still there, the website looks the same except for a small banner about a new hygienist — saved. *Dr. K's office.* In the phone. Tomorrow at nine-fifteen.

I notice I am already drafting the apology for the voicemail. *Hi, it's been a while, I'd like to schedule a cleaning.* I am going to keep the apology short. They do not need the year and a half. They just need the cleaning.

The list has *call dentist* on it for tomorrow, Monday. If I do it, I get to cross it off for the first time in eighteen months. If I don't, it migrates to Tuesday, and I will know, this time, that it migrated. The vote will be a vote I cast on purpose.

I want it to be the last migration. I am writing it down so I have to look at it tomorrow.

entries.prompt: Tell me about the last time you laughed at something you didn't actually find funny — a meeting, a friend's joke, something on a screen with someone in the room. Where were you, what was the laugh covering, and what would your face have done if you'd let it do whatever it wanted?
entries.content:
Wednesday night, Sam's college friend Nick was over for dinner. He comes through every few months when he's in town for work and the three of us do the same thing, which is wine and the long table and Nick telling stories. Nick is a good guy. I want to say that first. He is one of those people Sam has loved since they were nineteen and I can see why — he is generous, he remembers things about you, he asks the second question. He is also, I have come to understand, a person whose stories have a particular shape, which is that they end with him having said the perfect thing to someone who deserved it.

The story Wednesday was about a guy at his work. The guy had done something — I don't fully remember the setup, something about taking credit for a thing that wasn't his — and Nick had, in a meeting, in front of a senior person, delivered the line. I won't try to reproduce it. The line, in Nick's telling, was sharp. It landed. The senior person had, apparently, made eye contact with Nick afterward in a way that meant *good.* The guy had been quiet for the rest of the meeting.

Sam laughed. A real laugh. Sam loves Nick and loves these stories and was, I think, genuinely entertained.

I laughed too. I want to write down what kind of laugh it was because I caught it on the way out and I have been thinking about it since. It was the half-second-late laugh. It came out maybe a beat after Sam's, slightly louder than it needed to be to compensate for the lateness, with the small head-shake that says *oh man.* The laugh of a person who has registered that a laugh is required and is producing one to spec.

What I actually felt, hearing the story, was a small specific tiredness. Not at Nick. At the shape. The shape where the storyteller is the person who said the thing, and the other guy is the person who got told, and the senior person is the witness who confirms that the telling was correct. I have heard this shape from Nick maybe — I am being honest — fifteen times over the years. The details change. The shape does not. And what I noticed Wednesday, possibly because I have been writing for a while now about sentences that stay in the throat, is that I do not believe the shape. I do not believe that anyone, in a meeting, says the perfect line. Or — I believe it happens occasionally. I do not believe it happens to one person fifteen times. What I think happens, more often, is that you think of the line in the shower the next morning, and over time the shower-line migrates into the meeting in the telling, and after a few tellings it has always been in the meeting. I do this too. Everyone does this. I do not hold it against him.

But the laugh I gave him was a laugh that confirmed the shape. The laugh said *yes, you said it, and it was great, and the guy deserved it, and the senior person was right to nod.* The laugh was a small vote for the version of the story Nick was telling, which is the version where Nick is, again, the person who said the thing.

What was the laugh covering. A few things, stacked.

One — and this is the smallest, but it is real — I was a little bored. Not of Nick. Of the shape. I have been at the table for this story before and I knew, by the second sentence, where it was going, and the part of me that was bored had to be hidden because Sam was lit up and Nick was lit up and being bored at a friend's story while your partner is lit up is a small social crime. The laugh covered the boredom.

Two, I was a little envious. Not of Nick — of the move. Of the version of work where you say the line and the senior person nods. My version of work is the second conversation under the first, the bracing, the *I'd love to be less afraid of you,* the sentence that stays in the throat. Nick's version of work, in the telling, has no throat. The sentence comes out, lands, gets confirmed. I do not know if Nick's actual work is like this. I doubt it. But the telling produces a version where it is, and the version, for the length of the story, is in the room, and I am at the table holding a glass of wine wishing I were the kind of person who has stories with that shape. The laugh covered the envy.

Three, and this is the one I want to write down — I was uncomfortable with the guy. The other guy. The one in Nick's story who got told. Nick had described him in the way you describe the antagonist of a story you are telling at dinner, which is to say briefly and unfavorably. *This guy is a piece of work, he does this all the time.* I do not know that guy. I have no information about that guy except what Nick gave me, which is the information Nick needs me to have for the line to land. And I noticed, Wednesday, a small reluctance to laugh at him. Not a moral reluctance. Something quieter. A *I have been the person being described at someone else's dinner table, probably, and the description was probably not fair, and I would prefer the table not laugh at that description.* The laugh covered that too. The laugh said *yes, fair to render him this way, fair to laugh at the rendering.*

What my face would have done if I had let it do whatever it wanted. Probably nothing dramatic. A small *mm.* The dinner-table version of the *mm* I gave Jess's friend, the *mm* I gave my mom on the tile call before I caught myself. A noncommittal sound while I drank some wine. It would not have ruined the story. Nick would have kept going. Sam's laugh would have carried the room.

But it would have been mine. The face I had instead was Nick's face, the face the story wanted, and I gave it to him because he is Sam's friend and the dinner was warm and the wine was good and the cost of giving him the face was, in the moment, lower than the cost of not.

I want to be clear that I am not going to do anything about this. Nick is coming through again in February. I will laugh at the next story too. The laugh is the right size for the room and Nick deserves a warm room. What is changing, slowly, is just that I know what the laugh is doing while it is doing it. The sentence underneath is in the journal now. *I do not believe the shape, I am a little bored, I am a little envious, I do not want to laugh at the guy.* All four at once, a small stack, while the laugh did its work above them.

Sam, washing up after Nick left, said, "he's in a good mood lately." I said, "yeah." I did not say the rest. The rest is here.

The Critic.positiveIntent: Keep the day from going off the rails so I can be reliable for the people counting on me — Sam, Mei, the team.
The Critic.fearedOutcome: Letting something slip and watching Mei notice — the small flicker of "oh, you didn't" that I can't un-see.
The Critic.whatItProtects: A quieter part of me that goes blank when too many things are happening at once.
The Critic.userNotes: Loudest on Sunday evenings reviewing the week ahead, and on Monday mornings when the inbox loads. Quieter on Friday afternoons, almost absent on long walks.

The Deflector.positiveIntent: Make sure no one I care about — Sam, Riley, Mom on the phone — feels let down or under-prioritized.
The Deflector.fearedOutcome: Saying the wrong thing, or saying nothing, and losing the warmth of a friendship I depend on. Riley's text sitting unread for the third week.
The Deflector.whatItProtects: A part of me that just wants to be liked, that has a hard time when someone is even mildly disappointed.
The Deflector.userNotes: Says yes before the rest of me has caught up, especially to weekend invites. The resentment that arrives a day or two later is the tell.

The One Who Got Lucky.positiveIntent: Be safe. Not be the one called on without the answer.
The One Who Got Lucky.fearedOutcome: Being seen having forgotten — the specific sinking of being graded by someone whose opinion matters.
The One Who Got Lucky.whatItProtects: (itself — the youngest of the parts, the one the others are organized around keeping out of the room)
The One Who Got Lucky.userNotes: Surfaces when someone asks something completely benign about whether I did a thing. The grown-up voice answers in a second; this part has already been there for a few seconds.
